topics.title: Hydraulic Power Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET831 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Power Generators # ⚙️ 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hydraulic Power Generators**, in the context of fluid power engineering, are mechanical devices (commonly classified as **hydraulic pumps**) that convert **mechanical energy (rotational/kinetic input from a prime mover such as an electric motor or IC engine)** into **hydraulic energy (pressurized flow of hydraulic fluid)**. The pressurized fluid is then delivered to actuators, motors, or cylinders to perform useful mechanical work in industrial automation systems. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1), Hydraulic Power Generators are broadly classified into **two functional categories**: 1. **Non-Positive Displacement Pumps (Hydrodynamic Pumps):** Deliver fluid in a continuous, pulsation-free manner (e.g., Centrifugal pumps). Flow varies significantly with pressure. 2. **Positive Displacement Pumps (Hydrostatic Pumps):** Deliver a fixed volume of fluid per revolution, irrespective of system pressure (within rated limits). Flow is nearly constant, but pressure varies with load (e.g., Gear, Vane, Piston pumps). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **Positive Displacement Pumps** since they form the backbone of modern industrial hydraulic systems (machine tools, presses, injection moulding, mobile hydraulics). Centrifugal pumps are touched upon only for completeness and contrast. ### Key Performance Terminology | Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning | |------|--------|---------------|------------------| | Displacement Volume | $V_D$ | $\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ | Geometric volume swept per revolution | | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | $\text{LPM}$ (Litre/Min) | Actual fluid delivered per minute | | Pressure | $P$ | $\text{bar}$ or $\text{MPa}$ | Fluid force per unit area | | Rotational Speed | $N$ | $\text{rpm}$ | Pump shaft speed | | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th}$ | $\text{Nm}$ | Torque required for ideal pump | | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Ratio of output hydraulic power to input mechanical power | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" Model > [!NOTE] > **The Bucket Brigade Analogy:** > Imagine a group of workers (pump chambers) standing in a circular ring, each holding a bucket. As the ring rotates, every worker scoops a fixed quantity of water (displacement $V_D$) from a low-pressure sump and dumps it into a high-pressure delivery hose. The number of workers passing the discharge point per minute determines the **flow rate** ($Q$), while the height to which the water is forced defines the **pressure** ($P$). If some water leaks back through worn seals (volumetric losses) or friction slows the workers down (mechanical losses), the **efficiency** drops. This is precisely how a positive displacement hydraulic pump operates. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard reference values are universally used in KTU 2024 problem statements: - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ - **Standard oil density (ISO VG 46 mineral oil):** $\rho \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at $40^\circ\text{C}$ - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Conversion factors:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$, $1 \text{ LPM} = 10^{-3}/60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - **Recommended pump speeds for industrial gear pumps:** $N = 1000 \text{ to } 3000 \text{ rpm}$ # 🧠 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a Hydraulic Pump Works (Structured Logic) A positive displacement pump performs its function through a repeated, deterministic sequence: 1. **Suction / Intake Stroke:** The pump chamber (between meshing gears, sliding vanes, or reciprocating pistons) **expands in volume**, creating a local low-pressure zone (partial vacuum). Atmospheric pressure on the reservoir surface then **pushes hydraulic fluid into the chamber** through the inlet port. 2. **Fluid Confinement / Transport:** The fluid is mechanically **trapped within the chamber** (by gear teeth, vane tips, or piston seals) and is carried from the low-pressure inlet side to the high-pressure outlet side without escaping. 3. **Discharge / Compression Stroke:** The chamber **contracts in volume**, mechanically forcing the trapped fluid out through the outlet port against the system pressure (load). 4. **Cycle Repetition:** The process repeats continuously, producing a **pulsating (for piston pumps)** or **quasi-continuous (for gear/vane pumps)** flow of pressurized fluid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why pressure builds up:** In a positive displacement pump, the discharge fluid has nowhere to go except through the actuator (or a relief valve). Since the fluid is nearly incompressible, any resistance to flow manifests as a **rise in pressure**, governed by Pascal's Law: $P = F/A$. The pump therefore acts as a **constant-flow, variable-pressure** device. ## 2.2 Classification of Hydraulic Power Generators (KTU Syllabus Coverage) ### 2.2.1 Gear Pumps - **External Gear Pump:** Two identical meshing external gears rotate in a tight-clearance housing. Fluid is carried between the gear teeth and the housing wall. - **Internal Gear Pump:** Uses an external spur gear (rotor) and an internal ring gear (stator) with a crescent-shaped partition. ### 2.2.2 Vane Pumps - **Unbalanced Vane Pump:** Rotor with sliding vanes mounted eccentrically in a circular stator ring. - **Balanced Vane Pump:** Stator ring has a double-lobed (figure-8) profile, providing hydraulic radial force balance. ### 2.2.3 Piston Pumps - **Axial Piston Pump:** Pistons reciprocate parallel to the axis of rotation (swash plate or bent axis design). - **Radial Piston Pump:** Pistons reciprocate radially outward from a central cam ring. ### 2.2.4 Screw Pumps and other specialty variants Used in low-noise, high-pressure lubrication circuits. ## 2.3 Performance Parameters — The "Four Efficiencies" Framework ### 2.3.1 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$) Accounts for internal leakage from the high-pressure side back to the low-pressure side through clearances. $$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{V_D \cdot N}$$ ### 2.3.2 Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$) Accounts for frictional losses (bearing drag, viscous shearing, gear mesh friction). $$\eta_m = \frac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual}}$$ ### 2.3.3 Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$) The product of volumetric and mechanical efficiencies. $$\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$$ ### 2.3.4 Input Mechanical Power ($P_{in}$) Power drawn by the pump shaft from the prime mover. $$P_{in} = \frac{2 \pi N T_{actual}}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$ ### 2.3.5 Output Hydraulic Power ($P_{out}$) Useful power delivered to the hydraulic fluid. $$P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a healthy industrial gear pump, $\eta_v \approx 0.90$ to $0.95$ and $\eta_m \approx 0.90$ to $0.93$, giving $\eta_o \approx 0.85$ to $0.90$. Piston pumps achieve $\eta_o$ as high as $0.95$ to $0.97$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY FORMULA REFERENCE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS** | # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Variables | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|-----------|--------------------|-----------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Theoretical Flow Rate | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | $V_D$: displacement, $N$: rpm | $\text{cm}^3/\text{min}$ | Neglects leakage | | 2 | Actual Flow Rate | $Q_a = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | — | $\text{LPM}$ | $0 < \eta_v \le 1$ | | 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = Q_a / Q_{th}$ | — | Dimensionless | $0.85$ typical minimum | | 4 | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | $\Delta P$: pressure rise | $\text{Nm}$ | Per revolution | | 5 | Actual Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = T_{th} / \eta_m$ | — | $\text{Nm}$ | Includes friction | | 6 | Input Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | — | $\text{W}$ | Brake power required | | 7 | Output Hydraulic Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_a}{60}$ | $\Delta P$ in $\text{Pa}$, $Q_a$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$ | $\text{W}$ | Useful power | | 8 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta_v \eta_m$ | — | Dimensionless | $\le 1$ | | 9 | Specific Weight Conversion | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $h$: head (m) | $\text{Pa}$ | For hydrostatic problems | | 10 | Slip Coefficient | $S = Q_{th} - Q_a$ | — | $\text{LPM}$ | Leakage rate | > **Conversion tip:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$. When using $\Delta P$ in bar, $P_{out}[\text{kW}] = \dfrac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot Q[\text{LPM}]}{600}$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Hydraulic Power Generators are the **prime movers of fluid power systems** and are deployed in: - **Machine tool industries** (lathes, milling machines, CNC feed drives) — gear/vane pumps. - **Injection moulding machines** — high-pressure piston pumps. - **Mobile hydraulics** (excavators, loaders, tractors) — variable displacement axial piston pumps. - **Aerospace** (flight control actuators) — high-reliability radial piston pumps. - **Steel and metal forming presses** — extremely high-pressure piston pumps. - **Marine steering gear and deck machinery** — robust gear pumps. # 📐 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Torque of a Positive Displacement Pump ### Objective: To derive the expression for the theoretical torque required to drive a positive displacement pump against a pressure rise $\Delta P$. ### Step 1 — Set up the energy balance per revolution In one complete revolution, the pump displaces a geometric volume $V_D$ of fluid and raises its pressure by $\Delta P$. The hydraulic work done on the fluid per revolution is: $$W_{hydraulic, per\,rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$ Here, $\Delta P$ must be expressed in $\text{Pa}$ ($\text{N/m}^2$) and $V_D$ in $\text{m}^3$ to obtain work in Joules. ### Step 2 — Relate work to torque via the rotation angle By definition, the work done by a torque $T$ acting through one full revolution ($2\pi$ radians) is: $$W_{mechanical, per\,rev} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$$ ### Step 3 — Equate the two work expressions (ideal, lossless pump) Since the pump is theoretical (no losses), the mechanical input energy equals the hydraulic output energy: $$T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the theoretical torque $$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$ ### Step 5 — Include mechanical efficiency For a real pump, part of the input torque is lost to friction, so the actual shaft torque is: $$T_{sh} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check (Dimensional):** $[\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^3] = [\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^3] = [\text{N} \cdot \text{m}] = [\text{J}]$. Dividing by $[\text{rad}]$ (dimensionless angle) yields $[\text{Nm}] = [\text{Torque}]$. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Power Output from Flow and Pressure ### Step 1 — Define power as rate of work $$P_{out} = \frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{d(\Delta P \cdot V)}{dt} = \Delta P \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = \Delta P \cdot Q_a$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to practical engineering units For KTU problems, the following conversion chain is critical. When $\Delta P$ is in **bar** and $Q_a$ is in **LPM**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{out}[\text{W}] &= \Delta P[\text{Pa}] \times Q_a[\text{m}^3/\text{s}] \\ &= (\Delta P[\text{bar}] \times 10^5) \times \left(\frac{Q_a[\text{LPM}] \times 10^{-3}}{60}\right) \\ &= \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot Q_a[\text{LPM}] \times 10^2}{60} \\ &= \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot Q_a[\text{LPM}]}{0.6} \quad [\text{W}] \end{aligned} $$ To express in **kilowatts** (the KTU standard): $$P_{out}[\text{kW}] = \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot Q_a[\text{LPM}]}{600}$$ ### Step 3 — Derive the input mechanical power $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in}[\text{W}] &= \frac{2\pi N[\text{rpm}] \cdot T_{sh}[\text{Nm}]}{60} \\ &= \frac{2\pi N \cdot \Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot \eta_m} \\ &= \frac{N \cdot \Delta P \cdot V_D}{60 \cdot \eta_m} \end{aligned} $$ In **kW**: $$P_{in}[\text{kW}] = \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot V_D[\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}] \cdot N[\text{rpm}]}{600 \cdot \eta_m}$$ ### Step 4 — Cross-check overall efficiency $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_o &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_a}{60}}{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N}{60 \cdot \eta_m}} \\ &= \frac{Q_a \cdot \eta_m}{V_D \cdot N} \\ &= \frac{V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_m}{V_D \cdot N} \\ &= \eta_v \cdot \eta_m \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the fundamental relationship $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A hydraulic gear pump has a geometric displacement of $V_D = 25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. It runs at $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$ and delivers fluid at $\Delta P = 175 \text{ bar}$. If the volumetric efficiency is $\eta_v = 0.92$ and mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m = 0.90$, calculate: 1. Actual flow rate in LPM 2. Theoretical torque in Nm 3. Actual shaft torque in Nm 4. Input power in kW 5. Output hydraulic power in kW 6. Overall efficiency **Complete Step-by-Step Solution:** ### (1) Actual flow rate $Q_a$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_a &= V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v \\ &= 25 \times 1450 \times 0.92 \\ &= 33,350 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \\ &= 33.35 \text{ LPM} \end{aligned} $$ ### (2) Theoretical torque $T_{th}$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{th} &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} \\ &= \frac{(175 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}) \times (25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3)}{2\pi} \\ &= \frac{437.5}{6.2832} \\ &= 69.63 \text{ Nm} \end{aligned} $$ ### (3) Actual shaft torque $T_{sh}$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{sh} &= \frac{T_{th}}{\eta_m} = \frac{69.63}{0.90} \\ &= 77.37 \text{ Nm} \end{aligned} $$ ### (4) Input power $P_{in}$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in}[\text{kW}] &= \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot V_D \cdot N}{600 \cdot \eta_m} \\ &= \frac{175 \times 25 \times 1450}{600 \times 0.90} \\ &= \frac{6,343,750}{540} \\ &= 11,747.7 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 11.75 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ ### (5) Output hydraulic power $P_{out}$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{out}[\text{kW}] &= \frac{\Delta P[\text{bar}] \cdot Q_a[\text{LPM}]}{600} \\ &= \frac{175 \times 33.35}{600} \\ &= \frac{5836.25}{600} \\ &= 9.727 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ ### (6) Overall efficiency $\eta_o$ $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_o &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{9.727}{11.7477} \\ &= 0.8280 \\ &\approx 82.8\% \end{aligned} $$ **Verification using $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$:** $$\eta_o = 0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.828 \quad \checkmark$$ # 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Power Generator System The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of a hydraulic power generator system, illustrating how the prime mover, pump, fluid reservoir, and control elements interact to produce pressurized flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor IC Engine] --> B[COUPLING
Flexible Coupling] B --> C[HYDRAULIC PUMP
Positive Displacement Type] C --> D[DELIVERY MANIFOLD
High Pressure Line] D --> E[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE
System Safety] D --> F[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE
Flow Path Selection] F --> G[ACTUATOR BANK
Cylinders and Motors] G --> H[RETURN LINE
Low Pressure] H --> I[RESERVOIR
Oil Storage Tank] I --> J[FILTER
Contamination Control] J --> K[SUCTION LINE
Low Pressure Inlet] K --> C I --> L[HEAT EXCHANGER
Thermal Management] L --> I ``` ## 4.2 Classification Flow — Types of Hydraulic Power Generators ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HYDRAULIC POWER GENERATORS] --> B[POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT PUMPS] A --> C[NON POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT PUMPS] B --> D[GEAR PUMPS] B --> E[VANE PUMPS] B --> F[PISTON PUMPS] D --> D1[EXTERNAL GEAR] D --> D2[INTERNAL GEAR] E --> E1[UNBALANCED VANE] E --> E2[BALANCED VANE] F --> F1[AXIAL PISTON SWASH PLATE] F --> F2[AXIAL PISTON BENT AXIS] F --> F3[RADIAL PISTON] C --> C1[CENTRIFUGAL PUMP] C --> C2[AXIAL FLOW PUMP] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Energy Conversion Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT
Mechanical Power] --> B[PUMP ELEMENT] B --> C[OUTPUT
Hydraulic Power] A --> A1[Shaft Torque T in Nm] A --> A2[Rotational Speed N in rpm] C --> C1[Pressure P in bar] C --> C2[Flow Rate Q in LPM] B --> B1[VOLUMETRIC LOSS
Leakage across clearances] B --> B2[MECHANICAL LOSS
Friction in bearings and seals] B1 --> L1[Reduces Qa from Qth] B2 --> L2[Increases Tsh from Tth] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide for the diagrams:** > - **Diagram 4.1** shows the closed-loop power flow of a complete hydraulic circuit, highlighting that the pump does not consume the fluid — it merely pressurizes and recirculates it. > - **Diagram 4.2** is the **KTU 2024 exam-favorite** classification tree. Memorize the names and at least one distinguishing feature for each. > - **Diagram 4.3** illustrates the energy conversion losses that the four efficiency equations ($\eta_v, \eta_m, \eta_o$) quantify. # 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Note:** Each question is modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme Part A pattern — direct, definition/concept-focused, expected to be answered in 3–4 sentences with a small diagram or equation. --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define "Hydraulic Power Generator". Differentiate between positive and non-positive displacement pumps with suitable examples. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A Hydraulic Power Generator is a device that converts mechanical energy from a prime mover into hydraulic (pressurized fluid) energy. It is the heart of any fluid power system. | Feature | Positive Displacement | Non-Positive Displacement | |---------|----------------------|---------------------------| | Flow characteristic | Fixed per cycle, nearly independent of pressure | Varies inversely with pressure | | Pressure characteristic | Varies with system load | Relatively constant | | Examples | Gear, Vane, Piston pumps | Centrifugal, Axial flow pumps | **Suitability for hydraulics:** PD pumps are preferred in industrial hydraulics because of their high-pressure capability and precise flow control. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define volumetric efficiency of a hydraulic pump. State the typical range for a healthy industrial gear pump. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ is the ratio of the **actual flow rate** delivered by the pump to the **theoretical flow rate** based on its geometric displacement. It quantifies internal leakage losses. $$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{V_D \cdot N}$$ **Typical range for industrial gear pumps:** $\eta_v = 0.90$ to $0.95$ (90% to 95%). It decreases as system pressure rises, since higher pressure forces more fluid through internal clearances. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice (Option A or Option B) is mandatory. --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With the aid of a clear sketch, explain the construction and working of an **external gear pump**. State its typical applications, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic pump delivers $Q_a = 60 \text{ LPM}$ of oil at a discharge pressure of $180 \text{ bar}$. If the pump runs at $1440 \text{ rpm}$ with an overall efficiency of $85\%$, determine: (i) the volumetric efficiency, (ii) the input shaft power in kW, and (iii) the specific energy consumption. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to A(a): External Gear Pump** **Construction (3 marks):** - Two identical spur gears (drive and driven) are housed in a tightly toleranced casing. - The drive gear is coupled to the prime mover; the driven gear meshes externally. - Inlet and outlet ports are located on opposite sides of the meshing zone. - Side plates and end covers confine the fluid, with close clearances maintained. **Working (3 marks):** - On the un-meshed side, gear teeth move away from the meshing point, creating a low-pressure volume that draws oil from the inlet. - Oil is carried in the spaces between the gear teeth and the housing wall (peripheral pockets). - At the meshing point, teeth interlock and squeeze the oil out through the outlet port. - The meshing zone itself acts as a seal, preventing backflow. **Applications, Advantages, Limitations (1 mark):** - **Applications:** Machine tool lubrication, low-to-medium pressure hydraulic circuits (up to 200 bar), agricultural machinery, mobile equipment. - **Advantages:** Simple, compact, cheap, bidirectional, self-priming. - **Limitations:** Fixed displacement (unless complex design), noisy at high pressure, limited pressure capability due to gear-tooth load. --- #### **Model Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem** **Given:** $Q_a = 60 \text{ LPM}$, $\Delta P = 180 \text{ bar}$, $N = 1440 \text{ rpm}$, $\eta_o = 0.85$. We are NOT given $V_D$ directly. Using the relationship between $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$, and $\eta_o$: **(i) Volumetric efficiency is NOT directly computable** without knowing $V_D$. However, the input power and specific energy can be computed directly. *(Examiner note: Students often confuse $\eta_o$ with $\eta_v$.)* Wait — re-reading the problem, the standard KTU approach is to treat $\eta_o$ as given and compute input power. Volumetric efficiency is typically separately assumed, but here the problem may be ill-posed. We shall assume $\eta_v = 0.90$ and compute. **(i) Using the assumed $\eta_v = 0.90$** — this gives $Q_{th} = Q_a / \eta_v = 60 / 0.90 = 66.67 \text{ LPM}$. **(ii) Input shaft power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_o} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_a / 600}{\eta_o} \\ &= \frac{180 \times 60 / 600}{0.85} \\ &= \frac{18}{0.85} \\ &= 21.176 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Specific energy consumption (SEC):** $$ \begin{aligned} SEC &= \frac{P_{in}}{Q_a} = \frac{21.176 \text{ kW}}{60 \text{ LPM}} \\ &= 0.353 \text{ kW/LPM} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly compute $P_{in}$ using $\eta_v$ only (forgetting $\eta_o$ accounts for both losses). The **correct formula** is $P_{in} = P_{out} / \eta_o$. Also, do NOT confuse "overall efficiency" with "volumetric efficiency" — they differ by a factor of $\eta_m$. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]** **(a)** Compare the construction and performance of **axial piston pumps** (swash plate type) with **external gear pumps** under the following heads: pressure capability, efficiency, noise, cost, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An axial piston pump has a displacement of $V_D = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and runs at $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$ against a pressure of $350 \text{ bar}$. If the volumetric efficiency is $0.97$ and the mechanical efficiency is $0.95$, calculate: (i) actual flow rate in LPM, (ii) theoretical torque in Nm, (iii) input power in kW, and (iv) overall efficiency. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to B(a): Comparative Study (Tabular Form — 7 Marks)** | Parameter | External Gear Pump | Axial Piston Pump (Swash Plate) | |-----------|--------------------|----------------------------------| | **Pressure capability** | Up to 200 bar (moderate) | Up to 420 bar (very high) | | **Volumetric efficiency** | 0.88 – 0.95 | 0.95 – 0.98 | | **Mechanical efficiency** | 0.85 – 0.92 | 0.93 – 0.97 | | **Overall efficiency** | 0.80 – 0.88 | 0.90 – 0.95 | | **Noise level** | High (gear meshing) | Low to moderate (smooth fluid motion) | | **Cost** | Low (simple construction) | High (precision components) | | **Displacement control** | Fixed only | Easily variable (by tilting swash plate) | | **Typical applications** | Lubrication, low-pressure hydraulics, agricultural | High-pressure machine tools, presses, mobile hydraulics, aerospace | **Key engineering takeaway:** Piston pumps dominate **high-pressure, high-efficiency, variable-flow** applications; gear pumps dominate **low-cost, fixed-flow, low-pressure** applications. --- #### **Model Solution to B(b): Numerical Problem** **Given:** $V_D = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$, $\Delta P = 350 \text{ bar}$, $\eta_v = 0.97$, $\eta_m = 0.95$. **(i) Actual flow rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_a &= V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v \\ &= 50 \times 1500 \times 0.97 \\ &= 72,750 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \\ &= 72.75 \text{ LPM} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Theoretical torque:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{th} &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} \\ &= \frac{(350 \times 10^5) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} \\ &= \frac{1750}{6.2832} \\ &= 278.53 \text{ Nm} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Input power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in}[\text{kW}] &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N}{600 \cdot \eta_m} \\ &= \frac{350 \times 50 \times 1500}{600 \times 0.95} \\ &= \frac{26,250,000}{570} \\ &= 46,052.6 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 46.05 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Overall efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_o &= \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.97 \times 0.95 \\ &= 0.9215 \\ &\approx 92.15\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Unit conversion trap:** $V_D$ must be in $\text{m}^3$ (multiply cm³ by $10^{-6}$) when used with pressure in Pa. Forgetting this gives an answer off by a factor of $10^6$. > 2. **Sub-part (iv) does not need recomputation** from sub-part (iii) — the question is designed to test whether you know $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ as a standalone identity, not as a derived ratio. Many students waste 4–5 minutes recomputing via $P_{out}/P_{in}$ and risk arithmetic errors. > 3. **Pressure must be in absolute Pa** for theoretical torque. Convert 350 bar → 350 × 10⁵ Pa before substituting. --- ## 5.3 ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - [x] **Hydraulic Power Generators = Pumps** that convert mechanical energy → hydraulic energy. (Motors do the reverse.) - [x] **Two classes:** Positive Displacement (PD) and Non-Positive Displacement. **KTU focuses on PD pumps.** - [x] **PD pump types:** Gear (external/internal), Vane (balanced/unbalanced), Piston (axial/radial). - [x] **Gear pumps** = simple, cheap, fixed displacement, ≤ 200 bar, high noise. - [x] **Vane pumps** = moderate pressure (≤ 175 bar), balanced radial forces, quieter than gears. - [x] **Piston pumps** = high pressure (≥ 350 bar), highest efficiency, variable displacement via swash plate tilt. - [x] **Master formula quartet:** $Q_a = V_D N \eta_v$, $T_{sh} = \dfrac{\Delta P V_D}{2\pi \eta_m}$, $P_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta P Q_a}{60}$, $\eta_o = \eta_v \eta_m$. - [x] **Conversion constants:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ LPM} = 10^{-3}/60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $1 \text{ kW} = 600 \text{ bar·LPM}$ when using the $P_{out} = \Delta P Q / 600$ shortcut. - [x] **Typical efficiency benchmarks:** Gear $\eta_o \approx 85\%$, Vane $\eta_o \approx 88\%$, Piston $\eta_o \approx 92\%$. - [x] **Losses:** Volumetric loss = internal leakage; Mechanical loss = bearing/seal friction; Pressure loss = throttling at valves. - [x] **NEP 2020 / OBE alignment:** This topic maps primarily to **CO1** ("Understand the fundamental principles of hydraulic power generators") and **CO2** ("Analyze pump performance parameters"). Expect 2-mark definitions and 7-mark numerical problems. - [x] **Examiner's golden rule:** **Always state assumptions** (steady state, isothermal, incompressible fluid) and **always carry units** through every calculation step. KTU evaluators award partial marks for dimensional consistency even if the final number is wrong.

topics.title: Selection and Specification of Pumps, Pump Characteristics, Linear and Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET831 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection and Specification of Pumps, Pump Characteristics, Linear and Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydraulic Pumps: The "Heart" of the Hydraulic System

A **hydraulic pump** is a mechanical energy conversion device that converts the rotational kinetic energy of a prime mover (electric motor, diesel engine) into hydraulic energy (flow and pressure). It is classified fundamentally as a **hydrostatic positive displacement (PD) machine** because it displaces a fixed volume of fluid per revolution regardless of system back pressure (up to its rated limit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A hydraulic pump is a positive displacement device used to force a hydraulic fluid from its reservoir/case to the rest of the hydraulic system. It does NOT create pressure; pressure is created by the resistance to flow in the system (load).

**Conceptual Analogy (The Heart Analogy):**
Think of the pump as the **human heart**. The heart does not push blood with "force"; it simply displaces a fixed volume per beat. The "blood pressure" arises because narrow arteries (system resistance/load) resist the flow. If you connect a wide-open hose to the heart (no resistance), the pressure will be nearly zero, but the flow will be high. Similarly, a pump delivers constant **displacement per revolution**, and the system pressure depends on the load.

### 1.2 Hydraulic Actuators: The "Muscles" of the System

**Linear Actuators (Hydraulic Cylinders):** Convert hydraulic energy into linear mechanical force and motion. Used in presses, lifts, injection moulding machines, excavators, and aircraft landing gear.

**Rotary Actuators (Hydraulic Motors):** Convert hydraulic energy into rotary mechanical motion (torque and angular velocity). Used in winches, conveyor drives, drilling rigs, and steering systems.

> [!NOTE]
> A hydraulic motor is structurally a **pump in reverse**. A pump takes mechanical rotation and gives pressure-flow, while a motor takes pressure-flow and gives rotation.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pa (N/m²)** or **bar** | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa |
| Flow rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** or **L/min (lpm)** | 1 lpm = 6 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s |
| Force | $F$ | **N** or **kN** | 1 kN = 1000 N |
| Torque | $T$ | **N·m** | |
| Power | $P$ | **W (J/s)** or **kW** | 1 hp = 745.7 W |
| Displacement | $D$ or $V_D$ | **cm³/rev (cc/rev)** | |
| Viscosity | $\mu$ | **cSt (mm²/s)** | Kinematic viscosity |
| Speed (rotational) | $N$ | **rpm** | |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pump Classification (Foundation for Selection)

**A. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps** — Fixed volume per cycle.
- **External Gear Pump:** Two meshing external gears. Cheap, simple, noisy above 200 bar.
- **Internal Gear Pump:** Rotor + idler gear with crescent. Good for viscous fluids (up to 200 cSt).
- **Vane Pump:** Sliding vanes in an eccentric rotor. Quiet, balanced. Fixed or variable.
- **Piston Pump:** Axial or radial pistons. Highest efficiency, highest pressure (350–700 bar).
  - *Axial piston:* Bent-axis design, swash plate design.
  - *Radial piston:* Pistons arranged radially around a cam ring.

**B. Non-Positive Displacement Pumps (Centrifugal):** Flow varies inversely with pressure. Used in low-pressure cooling, lubrication, and transfer; NOT preferred in fixed-displacement industrial hydraulic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, focus on **PD pumps** (Gear, Vane, Piston). Centrifugal pumps are mentioned only for contrast.

### 2.2 Pump Selection — The 5-Specification Rule

Selection is based on five mandatory specifications (from any standard fluid power textbook, e.g., Majumdar / Esposito):

1. **Displacement per revolution ($V_D$)** — in cc/rev or cm³/rev. Determines the theoretical flow: $Q_T = V_D \times N \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s.
2. **Maximum continuous pressure rating ($P_{max}$)** — Must be ≥ Maximum Working Pressure × Safety Factor (typically 1.25).
3. **Maximum rotational speed ($N_{max}$)** — Pump must not be driven above this.
4. **Type of fluid & Viscosity range** — Mineral oil, water-glycol, synthetic. Recommended viscosity 16–40 cSt for most PD pumps.
5. **Mounting & Port connections** — SAE flange, BSP thread, cartridge type.

### 2.3 Pump Performance Parameters (Operational)

- **Theoretical Flow ($Q_T$):** $Q_T = V_D \times N$
- **Actual Flow ($Q_A$):** $Q_A = Q_T \times \eta_{vol}$
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$):** $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_A}{Q_T} \times 100\%$
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$):** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{T_T}{T_A} \times 100\%$
- **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{ov}$):** $\eta_{ov} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$
- **Pump Power Output (Hydraulic):** $P_{out} = P \times Q$
- **Pump Power Input (Shaft):** $P_{in} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$

### 2.4 Pump Characteristic Curves — The "Personality" of a Pump

A **pump characteristic curve** is a graph showing the relationship between the dependent variable (Flow, Power, Efficiency) on the Y-axis and the **Independent Variable (Pressure)** on the X-axis.

| Curve Type | Relationship | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Flow vs Pressure | $Q = Q_T - k \cdot P$ | Slight linear drop |
| Power vs Pressure | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{\eta}$ | Linear increase |
| Efficiency vs Pressure | $\eta_{ov}$ | Bell-shaped curve (peaks at rated pressure) |

**Three Standard Control Characteristics (for variable displacement pumps):**
1. **Constant Pressure (CP):** Pump maintains a fixed pressure; flow varies with demand. Used in presses.
2. **Constant Flow (CF):** Pump delivers fixed flow; pressure varies. Used in machine tools.
3. **Load Sensing (LS):** Pump delivers only the flow and pressure demanded by the load. Saves energy. Used in modern mobile hydraulics.

### 2.5 Actuator Fundamental Formulas (High-Yield)

**Linear Actuator (Cylinder):**

- **Cylinder Area (Piston side):** $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$
- **Annulus Area (Rod side):** $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- **Extension Force:** $F_{ext} = P \times A_p$
- **Retraction Force:** $F_{ret} = P \times A_r - F_{back}$
- **Extension Velocity:** $v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$
- **Retraction Velocity:** $v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_r}$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $A_r < A_p$, the **retraction force is lower** but the **retraction velocity is higher** for the same flow rate.

**Rotary Actuator (Hydraulic Motor):**

- **Theoretical Torque:** $T_T = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{2 \pi}$
- **Actual Torque:** $T_A = \dfrac{T_T}{\eta_{mech}}$
- **Theoretical Speed:** $N_T = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$
- **Actual Speed:** $N_A = N_T \times \eta_{vol}$
- **Power Output:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = P \times Q \times \eta_{ov}$

### 2.6 Affinity Laws (Variable Speed Operation of Pumps/Motors)

Used when the operating speed of a centrifugal pump or motor is changed. (For PD pumps, these are approximations but rarely used in KTU problems.)

$$\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$$

$$\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

$$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cube Law** ($P \propto N^3$) is a favorite KTU question: doubling the speed increases power consumption by 8 times.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern construction equipment (JCB, excavators) and injection moulding machines, **Load-Sensing piston pumps** are selected because they reduce energy wastage by 30–40% compared to constant-flow systems. Linear actuators are selected based on **buckling load** (Euler's formula) and **cushioning** requirements to avoid end-of-stroke shock.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Torque of a Hydraulic Motor

**Statement:** Show that the theoretical torque developed by a hydraulic motor is $T = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{2\pi}$.

**Step 1 — Setup the Energy Equivalence:**
By conservation of energy, the hydraulic power supplied to the motor equals the mechanical power output (assuming 100% efficiency at the theoretical level).

$$P_{hydraulic} = P_{mechanical}$$

**Step 2 — Express Hydraulic Power:**
Hydraulic power is the product of pressure and volumetric flow rate.

$$P_{hydraulic} = P \times Q \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3 — Express Mechanical Power:**
Mechanical power at the shaft is torque multiplied by angular velocity ($\omega$). For $N$ in rpm, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

$$P_{mechanical} = T \times \omega = T \times \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4 — Express Theoretical Flow:**
For a positive displacement motor, the theoretical flow in m³/s is displacement per revolution in m³ multiplied by speed in rev/s.

$$Q_T = V_D \times \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (3) into (1) and Equate to (2):**
Note: $V_D$ in cm³/rev must be converted to m³/rev by multiplying by $10^{-6}$. We keep the $10^{-6}$ for now.

$$P \times V_D \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{N}{60} = T \times \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel Common Terms:**
The $\dfrac{N}{60}$ term appears on both sides and cancels out. This is the key insight: **torque depends only on pressure and displacement, not on speed.**

$$P \times V_D \times 10^{-6} = T \times 2 \pi$$

**Step 7 — Solve for T:**
$$T = \dfrac{P \times V_D \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

If $V_D$ is in cc/rev and $P$ is in bar (where 1 bar = $10^5$ N/m²), the formula simplifies to:

$$T \text{ (N·m)} = \dfrac{P \text{ (bar)} \times V_D \text{ (cc/rev)}}{2 \pi \times 10} = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{62.83}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU Module 1. Memorize: $T = \dfrac{P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cylinder Force and Velocity Relationship

**Step 1 — Force Derivation from Pressure:**
Pressure is defined as force per unit area. $P = F/A \Rightarrow F = P \times A$.

For a piston with bore $D$, the piston-side area is $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_p = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity from Continuity:**
The flow entering the cylinder must equal the volume swept per second (continuity equation for incompressible fluid).

$$Q = A_p \times v \Rightarrow v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3 — The Critical Trade-off:**
Combining both:
- To double the force, double the pressure OR increase the bore by factor $\sqrt{2}$.
- To double the velocity, double the flow OR reduce bore by factor $\sqrt{2}$.

This creates a fundamental **Force-Velocity Trade-off** in hydraulic cylinder design.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of 50 cc/rev and operates at a pressure of 100 bar with an input flow of 60 lpm. The volumetric efficiency is 95% and the mechanical efficiency is 90%. Calculate:
(a) Theoretical Torque
(b) Actual Torque delivered
(c) Actual Speed
(d) Overall Efficiency
(e) Power Output

**Given:**
$V_D = 50$ cc/rev, $P = 100$ bar, $Q = 60$ lpm, $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

**Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Torque:**
$$T_T = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{2 \pi \times 10} = \dfrac{100 \times 50}{62.83} = 79.58 \text{ N·m}$$

**(b) Actual Torque:**
$$T_A = T_T \times \eta_m = 79.58 \times 0.90 = 71.62 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Theoretical Flow in cc/s:**
$$Q_T = 60 \text{ lpm} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{60} \text{ cc/s} = 1000 \text{ cc/s}$$

$$N_T = \dfrac{Q_T}{V_D} = \dfrac{1000}{50} = 20 \text{ rev/s} = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

$$N_A = N_T \times \eta_v = 1200 \times 0.95 = 1140 \text{ rpm}$$

**(d) Overall Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \times \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855 = 85.5\%$$

**(e) Power Output:**
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_A T_A}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1140 \times 71.62}{60} = 8548 \text{ W} = 8.55 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification using $P_{out} = P \times Q \times \eta_{ov}$:**
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^5 \times 60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \times 0.855 = 10000 \times 0.855 = 8550 \text{ W} \approx 8.55 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix (Pumps vs Applications)

| Application | Required Pressure | Recommended Pump Type | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Injection Moulding | 140–250 bar | Axial Piston (Variable) | High pressure, LS control |
| Excavator | 300–350 bar | Axial Piston (LS) | Energy efficient, high power density |
| Plastic Injection Unit | 100–200 bar | Radial Piston | Very high volumetric efficiency |
| Tractor Steering | 100–150 bar | Gear Pump | Cheap, moderate pressure |
| Log Splitter | 200 bar | Two-stage Gear + Piston | Low cost, intermittent duty |
| Machine Tool Coolant | 5–20 bar | Centrifugal (or Gear) | Low pressure, high flow |
| Mobile Crane | 280–320 bar | Bent-axis Piston | Compact, bi-directional |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic System with Pump and Actuators

The following Mermaid block illustrates how a pump delivers flow to a linear actuator and a rotary actuator in a typical industrial setup, governed by a directional control valve.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ReservoirA[Tank Reservoir] --> SuctionFilterA[Suction Strainer 100 micron]
    SuctionFilterA --> PumpNodeA[Fixed Displacement Pump V_D 50 cc rev]
    PumpNodeA --> CheckValveNodeA[Check Valve 5 bar crack pressure]
    CheckValveNodeA --> ReliefValveNodeA[Pressure Relief Valve set at 200 bar]
    CheckValveNodeA --> DCVNodeA[4 by 3 Directional Control Valve]
    DCVNodeA --> CylinderA[Double Acting Linear Cylinder Bore 100 mm Rod 60 mm]
    DCVNodeA --> MotorA[Hydraulic Motor V_D 80 cc rev Gear Type]
    CylinderA --> ReturnLineA[Return Line back to Tank]
    MotorA --> ReturnLineA
    ReturnLineA --> CoolerFilterA[Return Filter and Cooler]
    CoolerFilterA --> ReservoirA
    PumpNodeA -.drives.-> PrimeMoverA[Electric Motor 15 kW 1450 rpm]
    DCVNodeA --> LS_SignalA[Load Sensing Signal to Variable Pump]
    style ReservoirA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style PumpNodeA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style CylinderA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style MotorA fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

### 4.2 Pump Performance Characteristic Curves Schematic

This block-diagram shows how the three key performance metrics of a pump vary with operating pressure, which is essential for pump selection and system design.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PressAxisA[Pressure P bar] --> QFlowCurveA[Flow Q drops linearly with P]
    PressAxisA --> PowerCurveA[Power P_in rises linearly with P]
    PressAxisA --> EffCurveA[Efficiency eta is bell shaped peaks at rated P]
    QFlowCurveA --> QAxisA[Q axis]
    PowerCurveA --> PowerAxisA[Power axis]
    EffCurveA --> EffAxisA[eta axis percent]
    QFlowCurveA -- Q max at P zero --> OriginA[Origin]
    PowerCurveA -- intercept at zero --> OriginA
    EffCurveA -- low at low and high P --> OriginA
    style QFlowCurveA fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style PowerCurveA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style EffCurveA fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

### 4.3 Single-Acting vs Double-Acting Cylinder Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    SAC[Single Acting Cylinder] --> SAC_Feat[Spring return or gravity return]
    SAC_Feat --> SAC_App[Used in Jacks Lifts Clamps]
    DAC[Double Acting Cylinder] --> DAC_Feat[Power extend and power retract]
    DAC_Feat --> DAC_App[Used in Presses Excavators Injection units]
    DAC --> Cushion[End of stroke cushioning via tapered rod]
    Cushion --> Shock_Reduce[Reduces shock and vibration]
    style SAC fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style DAC fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
    style Cushion fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Pump Selection (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartA[Start Pump Selection] --> Q1A{Is pressure less than 200 bar}
    Q1A -- Yes --> Q2A{Is flow less than 100 lpm}
    Q1A -- No --> PistonNodeA[Select Axial Piston Pump]
    Q2A -- Yes --> Q3A{Is noise a concern}
    Q2A -- No --> GearNodeA[Select External Gear Pump]
    Q3A -- Yes --> VaneNodeA[Select Vane Pump]
    Q3A -- No --> GearNodeA
    PistonNodeA --> VDispA{Variable or Fixed displacement}
    VDispA -- Variable --> LSPistonA[Load Sensing Piston Pump]
    VDispA -- Fixed --> FixedPistonA[Fixed Displacement Piston Pump]
    style StartA fill:#e1f5ff
    style PistonNodeA fill:#fff9c4
    style GearNodeA fill:#c8e6c9
    style VaneNodeA fill:#ffccbc
    style LSPistonA fill:#d1c4e9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List any three factors considered in the selection of a hydraulic pump for an industrial application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Maximum operating pressure:** The pump's rated pressure must be at least 1.25 times the system's maximum working pressure to provide a safety margin. (1 mark)
2. **Required flow rate:** Determines the displacement $V_D$ using $V_D = Q / N$. The pump must deliver the theoretical flow at the chosen rotational speed. (1 mark)
3. **Type of hydraulic fluid and viscosity:** The pump design (gear, vane, piston) must match the fluid. Standard mineral oils require 16–40 cSt; water-glycols need special seals. (1 mark)

*(Bonus 1 mark possible for mentioning speed, temperature, mounting, or noise level.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a neat sketch. Mention one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Ports** | One port (cap end only) | Two ports (cap + rod end) |
| **Return Motion** | Spring / gravity / external load | Hydraulic pressure on rod side |
| **Force** | Only in one direction (extension) | Both directions (ext. and ret.) |
| **Application** | Hydraulic jack, lifting platform (1 mark) | Excavator boom, hydraulic press (1 mark) |
| **Sketch** | Piston rod comes out under pressure, returns via spring | Pressure alternates between cap and rod end (1 mark) |

---

### **Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Pump Selection and Performance**

**(a)** Explain the different types of positive displacement pumps used in industrial hydraulic systems, with neat sketches. Compare gear, vane, and piston pumps on the basis of pressure rating, efficiency, and noise. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Gear Pump (2 marks):** Two external gears mesh together; fluid is trapped between the gear teeth and the casing. It is carried from the inlet to the outlet port. Pressure rating up to **200 bar**, efficiency 85–90%, noisy above 150 bar, cheap, suitable for tractor steering and machine tool hydraulics.

**Vane Pump (2 marks):** A slotted rotor with sliding vanes rotates inside an elliptical cam ring. Fluid is carried in the spaces between the vanes. Pressure rating up to **175 bar**, efficiency 90–95%, quieter than gear, balanced design reduces vibration. Used in machine tools and injection moulding (low-noise zones).

**Piston Pump (2 marks):** Uses multiple pistons arranged axially (swash plate / bent axis) or radially around a central shaft. Pressure rating up to **700 bar** (axial), efficiency up to 97%, highest power density. Used in excavators, aircraft, and heavy presses.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Gear | Vane | Piston |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Pressure | 200 bar | 175 bar | 700 bar |
| Volumetric Efficiency | 90% | 95% | 97% |
| Noise Level | High | Low | Medium |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

**(b)** A fixed displacement pump has a displacement of 25 cc/rev and runs at 1450 rpm. The pump delivers oil at 150 bar with a volumetric efficiency of 92% and an overall efficiency of 85%. Calculate: **(i)** Actual flow delivered, **(ii)** Shaft power input, **(iii)** Mechanical efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_D = 25$ cc/rev, $N = 1450$ rpm, $P = 150$ bar, $\eta_v = 0.92$, $\eta_{ov} = 0.85$

**Solution:**

**(i) Actual Flow Delivered (2 marks):**
$$Q_T = V_D \times N = 25 \times 1450 = 36250 \text{ cc/min} = 36.25 \text{ lpm}$$
$$Q_A = Q_T \times \eta_v = 36.25 \times 0.92 = 33.35 \text{ lpm}$$

**(ii) Shaft Power Input (3 marks):**
Hydraulic Power Output:
$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{P \times Q_A}{600} = \dfrac{150 \times 33.35}{600} = 8.34 \text{ kW}$$
Shaft Power Input:
$$P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{ov}} = \dfrac{8.34}{0.85} = 9.81 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Efficiency (2 marks):**
$$\eta_m = \dfrac{\eta_{ov}}{\eta_v} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.92} = 0.924 = 92.4\%$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Linear and Rotary Actuators**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the construction and working of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. Derive the expressions for the extension and retraction forces and velocities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):** A double-acting cylinder consists of a cylinder barrel, a piston, a piston rod, two end caps (cap end and rod end), two port connections, seals (O-rings and wipers), and optional cushioning devices at both ends. The piston divides the cylinder into two chambers — the cap-end chamber (piston-side) and the rod-end chamber (annulus side).

**Working (1 mark):** Pressurized fluid enters the cap-end port during extension, pushing the piston outward. During retraction, pressurized fluid enters the rod-end port while the cap-end fluid is exhausted to the tank.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $D$ = piston bore, $d$ = rod diameter.

Piston-side area (cap end):
$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Annulus area (rod end):
$$A_r = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Extension force** (fluid in cap end, pressure $P$, no back pressure):
$$F_{ext} = P \times A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2 P}{4}$$

**Retraction force** (fluid in rod end, pressure $P$, back pressure $P_b$ in cap end):
$$F_{ret} = P \times A_r - P_b \times A_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2) P}{4} - \dfrac{\pi D^2 P_b}{4}$$

If $P_b = 0$:
$$F_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2) P}{4}$$

**Extension velocity** (flow $Q$ enters cap end):
$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Retraction velocity** (flow $Q$ enters rod end):
$$v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_r} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}$$

**Key Conclusion:** Since $A_p > A_r$, we have $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ but $v_{ext} < v_{ret}$ for the same flow.

---

**(b)** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of 80 cc/rev and receives a flow of 75 lpm at a pressure of 120 bar. If the volumetric efficiency is 96% and the mechanical efficiency is 92%, determine: **(i)** Actual speed, **(ii)** Theoretical torque, **(iii)** Actual torque, **(iv)** Power output in kW. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_D = 80$ cc/rev, $Q = 75$ lpm, $P = 120$ bar, $\eta_v = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.92$

**Solution:**

**(i) Actual Speed (2 marks):**
$$Q_T = 75 \text{ lpm} = 75000 \text{ cc/min}$$
$$N_T = \dfrac{Q_T}{V_D} = \dfrac{75000}{80} = 937.5 \text{ rpm}$$
$$N_A = N_T \times \eta_v = 937.5 \times 0.96 = 900 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Theoretical Torque (2 marks):**
$$T_T = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{2\pi \times 10} = \dfrac{120 \times 80}{62.83} = 152.79 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Actual Torque (1 mark):**
$$T_A = T_T \times \eta_m = 152.79 \times 0.92 = 140.57 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) Power Output (2 marks):**
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_A T_A}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 900 \times 140.57}{60} = 13247 \text{ W} = 13.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification (extra credit):**
$$P_{out} = P \times Q \times \eta_v \times \eta_m = \dfrac{120 \times 10^5 \times 75 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \times 0.96 \times 0.92 = 13248 \text{ W} \approx 13.25 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion in the torque formula:** The constant 62.83 is for $V_D$ in cc/rev and $P$ in bar. Using $P$ in Pa requires $V_D$ in m³/rev. Mixing units is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Confusing lpm with m³/s:** 1 lpm = $10^{-3}/60$ m³/s. Forgetting this gives a 60× error in power.
> 3. **Cylinder area sign convention:** Students often write $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ for the rod side, forgetting to subtract the rod area. This makes retraction force *greater* than extension — physically impossible.
> 4. **Volumetric efficiency confusion:** $\eta_v = Q_A / Q_T$ (not $Q_T / Q_A$). Mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m = T_T / T_A$.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety factor:** In selection questions, a pump rated 200 bar cannot be used at 200 bar continuously — must be ≥ 1.25 × working pressure.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)**

- **Pump is NOT a pressure source** — it is a flow source. Pressure is a system response to load.
- **5 Selection Criteria:** Displacement, Pressure rating, Speed, Fluid type, Mounting.
- **Three PD Pump Types:** Gear (≤200 bar, cheap, noisy), Vane (≤175 bar, quiet), Piston (≤700 bar, premium).
- **Pump Characteristic Curve axes:** Always plot **independent variable (P) on X-axis** and **dependent (Q, Power, η) on Y-axis**.
- **Bell-shaped efficiency curve** peaks at the rated pressure — running below or above reduces efficiency.
- **Three pump control modes:** Constant Pressure, Constant Flow, **Load Sensing (LS)**.
- **Torque formula (motor):** $T = \dfrac{P \times V_D}{2\pi \times 10}$ [N·m, bar, cc/rev]. **Speed independent of pressure.**
- **Cylinder Force:** $F = P \times A$. **Velocity:** $v = Q / A$.
- **Cylinder Force-Velocity Trade-off:** $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$, but $v_{ext} < v_{ret}$ for the same $Q$ and $P$.
- **Affinity Law — Cube Law:** Power $\propto N^3$ (favorite 7-mark derivation question).
- **Volumetric losses** = internal leakage (reduce flow). **Mechanical losses** = friction (reduce torque).
- **Cushioning** is mandatory in cylinders with stroke > 500 mm or speeds > 0.5 m/s to prevent end-of-stroke shock.
- **Most common KTU mistake:** Confusing the pump formula $Q = V_D \cdot N \cdot 10^{-6}$ (where $V_D$ is in cc/rev and $Q$ is in m³/s) with the unit-less version. Always write units.
- **Application memory trick:** *“Gear for Tractor, Vane for Tools, Piston for Power.”*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET831 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Pressure

In the context of **Industrial Hydraulics and Automation**, **Pressure** is formally defined as the **normal force exerted per unit area** by a fluid on a surface (real or imaginary) in contact with it. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the perpendicular component of force to the area over which it acts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure is the fundamental scalar state variable governing all hydraulic and pneumatic power transmission systems. Every design calculation in this module (pumps, valves, actuators, and circuits) begins with a pressure analysis.

Pressure is a **scalar quantity** with units of **Newton per square meter (N/m²)**, which is defined as the **Pascal (Pa)** in the SI system. The symbol used universally is **p** (lowercase), and it is the most critical thermodynamic property of a static fluid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Inflated Balloon"

Imagine pressing your thumb against a fully inflated balloon. Your thumb applies a force, but the balloon's surface curves away. Now imagine pressing a thumbtack against the same balloon with the *same force*. The pin pierces it immediately. What changed? Not the force — the **area** shrank dramatically. The smaller the contact area, the higher the pressure.

In hydraulic systems, this principle is harnessed in reverse: a tiny force on a small piston creates a huge force on a large piston because the **pressure** is conserved. Think of a doctor's syringe — pushing the plunger gently with your finger creates enough pressure to inject fluid through a needle. Hydraulic presses multiply this effect by thousands of times to bend steel.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

The following standard reference values are used universally in KTU-level hydraulic calculations and **must be memorized**:

| Reference Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$) | **101,325 Pa** or **1.01325 bar** |
| Density of Water at 4°C ($\rho_{water}$) | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to Gravity ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Standard Temperature | **288.15 K** (15°C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter:** Atmospheric pressure is the *reference datum* for all pressure measurement. Without knowing $P_{atm}$, we cannot distinguish between absolute and gauge readings — a common source of numerical errors in KTU exam problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Pressure

The behavior of pressure in a hydraulic fluid is governed by three foundational principles, each of which carries independent KTU exam weightage:

#### 2.1.1 Pascal's Law (The Foundation of Hydraulics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to any part of a confined fluid at rest is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts **perpendicular** to the surfaces of the containing vessel.

**Why this is the heart of hydraulics:** It enables force multiplication. A small input force on a small piston can lift enormous loads when applied through a large piston, because the *pressure* (not the force) is constant.

#### 2.1.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

For a static incompressible fluid, the pressure at any depth $h$ below a free surface is determined solely by the weight of the fluid column above it. This yields the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$P = P_0 + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P$ = Total (absolute) pressure at depth $h$
- $P_0$ = Pressure acting on the free surface (often atmospheric)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity
- $h$ = Vertical depth measured from the free surface

#### 2.1.3 The Continuity of Pressure (Continuity Equation for Pressure)

In a static fluid, the pressure at any two points at the same horizontal elevation must be equal, regardless of the shape of the container. This is the principle behind manometers and U-tube measurements.

### 2.2 Classification of Pressure — The KTU High-Yield Framework

Pressure in industrial hydraulics is classified into **four** distinct categories, each serving a different measurement and control purpose:

1. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference). It is the true thermodynamic pressure.
2. **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured *above* atmospheric pressure. This is what most industrial dial gauges display. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$
3. **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Measured *below* atmospheric pressure. Used when suction conditions exist. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$
4. **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$):** The difference in pressure between any two points in a system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**A**bsolute is **A**ll; **G**auge is **G**reater than Atmosphere." When a gauge reads zero, the absolute pressure is still 1.01325 bar.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Pressure Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Standard SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure from force/area | Pa = N/m² |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge to absolute conversion | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute to gauge conversion | Pa |
| 5 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure definition | Pa |
| 6 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Pascal's Law (force/area balance) | N/m² |
| 7 | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ | Bar to Pascal conversion | Pa |
| 8 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ | Standard atmosphere | Pa |
| 9 | $1 \text{ psi} = 6894.76 \text{ Pa} = 0.0689476 \text{ bar}$ | Pound per square inch | Pa |
| 10 | $1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 = 0.980665 \text{ bar}$ | Metric technical atmosphere | bar |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of pressure underpins virtually every industrial hydraulic application:

- **Hydraulic Presses and Lifts:** A 10 N input force on a 1 cm² piston generates 10 bar of pressure, which can lift a 10,000 kg mass on a 100 cm² piston. This is the working principle of **Pascal's Law** in action.
- **Braking Systems (Automotive):** Hydraulic brake pedals transmit driver foot pressure equally to all four wheel cylinders, ensuring synchronized braking force.
- **Pressure Vessels and Boilers:** Industrial pressure vessels are designed using the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, with safety factors tied to maximum allowable working pressure.
- **Pneumatic Control Systems:** Pressure regulators in pneumatic circuits maintain downstream pressure within tight tolerances (typically ±0.1 bar) for precision actuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Perspective:** Every pump specification, valve rating, and actuator selection in the OEMET831 syllabus begins with a pressure specification. Understanding pressure is the foundational competency upon which all subsequent modules (Hydraulic Circuits, Valves, Actuators) are built.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation ($P = \rho g h$)

**Statement to prove:** The pressure at the bottom of a static fluid column of height $h$ and density $\rho$ is $P = \rho g h$ (above atmospheric).

**Step 1 — Define a control volume:**
Consider a vertical column of fluid with a uniform cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$, standing on a horizontal surface.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces acting on the column:**
Three vertical forces act on the fluid column:
- The weight of the fluid column pulling it downward: $W = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = \rho \cdot (A \cdot h) \cdot g$
- The atmospheric pressure pushing down on the top surface: $F_{top} = P_{atm} \cdot A$
- The reaction pressure pushing up on the bottom surface: $F_{bottom} = P \cdot A$

**Step 3 — Apply static equilibrium:**
Since the fluid is at rest, the sum of vertical forces must equal zero (no acceleration):

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

$$P \cdot A - P_{atm} \cdot A - W = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**
Substituting $W = \rho A h g$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$P \cdot A - P_{atm} \cdot A - \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g = 0$$

Divide through by $A$:

$$P - P_{atm} - \rho g h = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for P:**

$$\boxed{P = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

This is the **complete hydrostatic pressure equation**. If the surface is open to the atmosphere, $P_{atm}$ is the reference; if it is sealed, $P_{atm}$ is replaced by the actual pressure on the free surface.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pascal's Law and Hydraulic Press Force Multiplication

**Given:** A hydraulic system with a small piston (input side) of area $A_1$ and a large piston (output side) of area $A_2$, both connected by an incompressible fluid in a sealed U-tube system.

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law at the fluid interface:**
Since both pistons are in contact with the same continuous fluid body, the pressure transmitted through the fluid must be equal at both pistons.

$$P_1 = P_2$$

**Step 2 — Express pressure as force per unit area:**

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3 — Define the mechanical advantage (MA):**
The force multiplication factor is the ratio of the output force to the input force:

$$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply volume conservation (incompressibility):**
The volume of fluid displaced by the small piston must equal the volume gained by the large piston:

$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

Where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the displacements of pistons 1 and 2 respectively. This means the output piston moves a **shorter distance** than the input piston — energy is conserved at the cost of displacement.

**Step 5 — Final boxed relationship:**

$$\boxed{\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

This pair of equations is the **theoretical heart of every hydraulic press, jack, and brake system**.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 5 cm and a large piston of diameter 50 cm. A force of 200 N is applied on the small piston. Calculate:
> (a) The pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid (in bar).
> (b) The load that can be lifted by the large piston.
> (c) The displacement of the large piston when the small piston moves 20 cm.

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of small piston, $d_1 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Diameter of large piston, $d_2 = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- Input force, $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$
- Input displacement, $x_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.50)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the developed pressure:**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P = 101,859 \text{ Pa} = 1.01859 \text{ bar} \approx 1.02 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the lifting force on the large piston:**
Using Pascal's Law ($P_1 = P_2$):

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 101,859 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-1}$$

$$F_2 = 20,000 \text{ N} = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the displacement of the large piston:**
Using volume conservation:

$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 0.20 \times \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$x_2 = 0.002 \text{ m} = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification (Energy Conservation Check):**

$$\text{Work Input} = F_1 \cdot x_1 = 200 \times 0.20 = 40 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{Work Output} = F_2 \cdot x_2 = 20,000 \times 0.002 = 40 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{Work Input} = \text{Work Output} = 40 \text{ J} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Note how the force was multiplied by 100 (from 200 N to 20,000 N), but the displacement was divided by 100 (from 20 cm to 2 mm). This is the **fundamental trade-off in all hydraulic systems**: you gain force but lose displacement. Energy (the product of force and displacement) is conserved.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pressure Measurement System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a generic industrial pressure measurement loop, showing how a physical pressure signal is converted, transmitted, and displayed:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Pressure Source] --> P2[Pressure Sensor Element]
    P2 --> P3[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    P3 --> P4[Analog to Digital Converter]
    P4 --> P5[Microprocessor or Indicator]
    P5 --> P6[Digital Display Output]
    P5 --> P7[Alarm or Control Output]
    P7 --> P8[Actuator or Shutdown Relay]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **P1** represents the physical pressure being measured (e.g., hydraulic line, gas cylinder, boiler).
- **P2** is the primary sensing element (Bourdon tube, diaphragm, strain gauge, or piezoelectric crystal).
- **P3** conditions the low-level signal (amplification, filtering, linearization).
- **P4** converts the analog voltage/current to a digital number.
- **P5** is the brain — it scales, calibrates, and computes the displayed value.
- **P6** shows the result on a digital readout.
- **P7 and P8** close the control loop by triggering alarms or shutdown actuators when setpoints are exceeded.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Pressure Types and Reference Relationships

This diagram maps the conceptual relationships between absolute, gauge, and vacuum pressures — a high-frequency KTU question topic:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VAC[Absolute Zero Reference]
    VAC --> ABS[Absolute Pressure Pabs]
    ATM[Atmospheric Pressure Patm = 101.325 kPa]
    ATM --> GAUGE[Gauge Pressure Ppositive above atm]
    ATM --> VACUUM[Vacuum Pressure Pbelow atm]
    ABS --> REL1[Relation Pabs equals Patm plus Pgauge]
    ABS --> REL2[Relation Pabs equals Patm minus Pvac]
    GAUGE --> REL1
    VACUUM --> REL2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Industrial Pressure Measuring Instruments

The following diagram categorizes the principal pressure-measuring instruments used in KTU-referenced industrial applications:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PM[Pressure Measuring Instruments]
    PM --> M[Manometers]
    PM --> E[Elastic Deformation Gauges]
    PM --> ELEC[Electronic Transducers]
    PM --> CAL[Calibration Standards]
    M --> M1[U Tube Manometer]
    M --> M2[Inclined Tube Manometer]
    M --> M3[Well Type Manometer]
    E --> E1[Bourdon Tube Gauge]
    E --> E2[Diaphragm Gauge]
    E --> E3[Bellows Gauge]
    ELEC --> EL1[Strain Gauge Type]
    ELEC --> EL2[Piezoresistive Type]
    ELEC --> EL3[Capacitive Type]
    ELEC --> EL4[Piezoelectric Type]
    CAL --> C1[Dead Weight Tester]
    CAL --> C2[Master Gauge Comparison]
```

**Instrument Selection Guidance for KTU:**
- **Manometers:** Low-pressure, high-accuracy laboratory and calibration use. Best for pressures below 1 bar.
- **Bourdon Tube Gauges:** The industrial workhorse. Used for 0–700 bar range in hydraulic systems. Robust and inexpensive.
- **Diaphragm Gauges:** Preferred for viscous, corrosive, or slurry fluids. Used in chemical and food processing.
- **Strain Gauge / Piezoresistive:** Electronic systems for data acquisition, SCADA, and PLC input.
- **Dead Weight Tester:** The *primary standard* for calibrating all other pressure gauges. Generates known pressure from calibrated weights.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Practical Reference:** In OEMET831 laboratory sessions, students typically calibrate a Bourdon tube gauge against a dead weight tester. The Bourdon tube principle is named after French engineer Eugène Bourdon (1849 patent). When internal pressure rises, the flattened C-shaped tube tends to straighten, and this small displacement is amplified through a gear mechanism to rotate the pointer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with SI units: (i) Absolute pressure, (ii) Gauge pressure, (iii) Vacuum pressure.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Absolute Pressure:** The pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (zero pressure reference). It is the true thermodynamic pressure of a fluid. **SI Unit: Pascal (Pa) or N/m².** Formula: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$

**(ii) Gauge Pressure:** The pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. It is positive for pressures above atmospheric and negative for pressures below atmospheric. Most industrial dial gauges read gauge pressure. **SI Unit: Pascal (Pa).** Formula: $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$

**(iii) Vacuum Pressure:** The amount by which the absolute pressure falls *below* atmospheric pressure. It indicates the degree of vacuum and is always a positive quantity. **SI Unit: Pascal (Pa).** Formula: $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
State Pascal's Law. How is it applied in a hydraulic press?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Pascal's Law:** "Pressure applied to any part of a confined fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel."

**Application in Hydraulic Press:** A hydraulic press consists of two connected cylinders of different cross-sectional areas ($A_1$ small, $A_2$ large) filled with incompressible hydraulic fluid. A small input force $F_1$ applied to the smaller piston generates pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$. By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted equally to the larger piston, producing an output force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2 / A_1)$. This provides mechanical advantage and is used in forging, sheet metal forming, and compression molding.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = \rho g h$ for a static incompressible fluid. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Bourdon tube pressure gauge is used to measure the pressure of oil in a hydraulic line. The gauge reads 45 bar. If the local atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar, calculate: (i) the absolute pressure in the line in bar, (ii) the absolute pressure in Pa, (iii) the gauge reading if the same line were located in a high-altitude facility where atmospheric pressure is 0.75 bar. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is incompressible (constant density $\rho$).
2. The fluid is at rest (no shear stress, only normal pressure stresses).
3. Acceleration due to gravity $g$ is constant over the fluid column.

**Derivation:**

Consider a small fluid element of area $dA$ and height $dh$ at depth $h$ from the free surface. The forces acting on this element in the vertical direction are:
- Pressure $P$ acting upward on the bottom face: $F_{up} = P \cdot dA$
- Pressure $(P + dP)$ acting downward on the top face: $F_{down} = (P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Weight of the element pulling downward: $dW = \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh$

For static equilibrium, $\sum F_{vertical} = 0$:

$$P \cdot dA - (P + dP) \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh = 0$$

$$-dP \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh = 0$$

$$dP = -\rho \cdot g \cdot dh$$

Integrating from the free surface (where $h = 0$ and $P = P_0$) to depth $h$ (where $P = P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P = P_0 - \rho g h \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad P = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

The negative sign in the differential equation indicates that pressure *increases* with depth.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Gauge pressure reading: $P_{gauge} = 45 \text{ bar}$
- Local atmospheric pressure (sea level): $P_{atm,1} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$

**(i) Absolute pressure in bar:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 1.013 + 45 = 46.013 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Absolute pressure in Pa:**

$$P_{abs} = 46.013 \text{ bar} \times 10^5 \text{ Pa/bar} = 4.6013 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**(iii) Gauge reading at high altitude:**
The absolute pressure in the line does not change (it is determined by the fluid system, not the atmosphere). Therefore:

$$P_{abs} = 46.013 \text{ bar (unchanged)}$$

At the high-altitude facility, the *gauge* reading is:

$$P_{gauge,2} = P_{abs} - P_{atm,2} = 46.013 - 0.75 = 45.263 \text{ bar}$$

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge with a neat sketch. List any three advantages and two disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system operates at a working pressure of 200 bar. The system has a single-acting cylinder with a piston diameter of 100 mm. Calculate: (i) the thrust force developed by the cylinder in kN, (ii) the volume of oil displaced per stroke in liters if the stroke length is 250 mm, (iii) the pump delivery rate required to complete 10 working strokes in 30 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Bourdon Tube Working Principle:**

A Bourdon tube is a flattened C-shaped or helical metallic tube with one end sealed and the other end open to the pressure source. When fluid under pressure enters the open end, the internal pressure tries to straighten the curved tube. This small displacement at the free end is transmitted through a mechanical linkage (lever and gear mechanism) to rotate the pointer on a calibrated dial.

**Sketch (Block Equivalent):**

```
Pressure Inlet --> [Bourdon Tube - C-Section]
                         |
                         v
              [Link and Gear Mechanism]
                         |
                         v
                  [Rotating Pointer]
                         |
                         v
                  [Calibrated Dial Scale]
```

**Three Advantages:**
1. Simple, robust construction suitable for harsh industrial environments.
2. Wide measurement range (from vacuum up to 7000 bar with appropriate materials).
3. Good accuracy (typically ±0.5% to ±2% of full scale) at low cost.

**Two Disadvantages:**
1. Susceptible to mechanical shock and vibration, which can damage the gearing.
2. Hysteresis error occurs due to elastic fatigue of the tube material over time.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Working pressure: $P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- Piston diameter: $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Stroke length: $L = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Number of strokes: $N = 10$
- Total time: $T = 30 \text{ s}$

**(i) Thrust force developed:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F = P \cdot A = (2 \times 10^7) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F = 1.5708 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 157.08 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Volume displaced per stroke:**

$$V_{stroke} = A \cdot L = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.25 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Converting to liters ($1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$):

$$V_{stroke} = 1.9635 \text{ L per stroke}$$

**(iii) Pump delivery rate:**

Total volume required for 10 strokes:

$$V_{total} = 10 \times 1.9635 = 19.635 \text{ L}$$

Pump delivery rate:

$$Q = \frac{V_{total}}{T} = \frac{19.635 \text{ L}}{30 \text{ s}} = 0.6545 \text{ L/s}$$

Converting to LPM (liters per minute):

$$Q = 0.6545 \times 60 = 39.27 \text{ LPM}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently mix units (e.g., using cm² instead of m² with N). Always convert to SI base units *first* before substituting into formulas. **Loses 1–2 marks typically.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Atmospheric Pressure:** When a problem states "gauge reads X bar," many students treat that as absolute pressure. Always check whether atmospheric pressure must be added. **Loses 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Confusing Diameter and Radius in Area Calculations:** Using diameter squared directly (instead of radius squared or $D^2/4$) is the single most common error in piston/cylinder problems. **Loses 2 marks easily.**
>
> 4. **Sign Convention Errors in Vacuum Calculations:** Vacuum pressure is always reported as a *positive* number (it's a deficit, not a signed quantity). Students often write negative values and lose marks. **Loses 1 mark.**
>
