State any two limitations. **Model Answer:** The cascade method is a **pneumatic-only** technique for designing sequential circuits. It uses **groups of cylinders** with each group having a dedicated **memory pair (two 3/2 way valves)** that remembers which group is currently active. When the last step of a group finishes, a limit valve signal **resets the current group** and **sets the next group**. **Limitations:** 1. Number of steps is **limited by the number of memory valves** (each new group adds 2 memory valves). 2. The method becomes **impractical for sequences longer than 6–8 steps** due to hardware explosion. 3. It is **not easily modifiable** — adding a new step may require complete re-grouping. > [!NOTE] > **Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] ### Question 2: Differentiate between Mapping Method and Step Counter Method. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Mapping Method | Step Counter Method | |---|---|---| | Memory element | Boolean equations | Digital binary counter | | Hardware | AND/OR valves | Counter + Decoder IC | | Expandability | Medium | High | | Power supply | Air (or electric) | Electric + Air | | Max steps | Unlimited (theoretical) | $2^n$ (where n = counter bits) | > [!NOTE] > **Tag:** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Design Using Cascade Method (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** > Design a cascade sequential circuit for the sequence **A+ B+ A− B+ B− A−** with appropriate cylinder grouping. Draw the cascade circuit and the power circuit. List all pilot signals used. **(a)** Draw the **displacement-step diagram** and identify the groups. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Draw the **cascade pair circuit** and the **power circuit** showing all solenoid connections. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Displacement-Step Diagram & Grouping** The sequence is A+ B+ A− B+ B− A− (6 steps, 2 cylinders). | Step | Motion | Cylinders Active | End Signal | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A+ | A | $a_1$ | | 2 | B+ | B | $b_1$ | | 3 | A− | A | $a_0$ | | 4 | B+ | B | $b_1$ | | 5 | B− | B | $b_0$ | | 6 | A− | A | $a_0$ | **Grouping:** With $k = 2$ cylinders per group (or 2 steps per group as per 2024 convention): $$G = \lceil 6 / 2 \rceil = 3 \text{ groups}$$ - **Group 1:** Steps 1 & 2 (A+, B+) — end signal: $b_1$ - **Group 2:** Steps 3 & 4 (A−, B+) — end signal: $b_1$ - **Group 3:** Steps 5 & 6 (B−, A−) — end signal: $a_0$ **Memory valves needed:** $$M = 2 \times G = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ memory valves (3 pairs)}$$ **Pilot Signal Equations:** For Group 1 (1a set, 1b reset): - $1S_1 = (1a) \cdot a_0$ → A+ in Step 1 - $1S_2 = (1a) \cdot a_1$ → B+ in Step 2 - Reset 1a, Set 2a at $b_1$ (end of Group 1) For Group 2 (2a set, 2b reset): - $2S_1 = (2a) \cdot b_1$ → A− in Step 3 - $2S_2 = (2a) \cdot a_0$ → B+ in Step 4 - Reset 2a, Set 3a at $b_1$ (end of Group 2) For Group 3 (3a set, 3b reset): - $3S_1 = (3a) \cdot b_1$ → B− in Step 5 - $3S_2 = (3a) \cdot b_0$ → A− in Step 6 - Reset 3a, Set 1a at $a_0$ (cycle restart) **Part (b) — Cascade Circuit & Power Circuit** ```mermaid flowchart TD StartBtn["START a0"] --> G1Set["1a SET"] G1Set --> G1Memory["Group 1 Memory 1a 1b"] G1Memory --> Val1S1["1S1 = 1a AND a0
A+ Solenoid"] G1Memory --> Val1S2["1S2 = 1a AND a1
B+ Solenoid"] Val1S1 --> CyAplus["Cylinder A Extends"] Val1S2 --> CyBplus1["Cylinder B Extends Step 2"] CyBplus1 --> LimB1["b1 generated"] LimB1 --> G1Reset["1b RESET Group 1"] LimB1 --> G2Set["2a SET Group 2"] G2Set --> G2Memory["Group 2 Memory 2a 2b"] G2Memory --> Val2S1["2S1 = 2a AND b1
A- Solenoid"] G2Memory --> Val2S2["2S2 = 2a AND a0
B+ Solenoid"] Val2S1 --> CyAminus["Cylinder A Retracts"] Val2S2 --> CyBplus2["Cylinder B Extends Step 4"] CyBplus2 --> LimB1b["b1 again"] LimB1b --> G2Reset["2b RESET Group 2"] LimB1b --> G3Set["3a SET Group 3"] G3Set --> G3Memory["Group 3 Memory 3a 3b"] G3Memory --> Val3S1["3S1 = 3a AND b1
B- Solenoid"] G3Memory --> Val3S2["3S2 = 3a AND b0
A- Solenoid"] Val3S1 --> CyBminus["Cylinder B Retracts"] Val3S2 --> CyAminus2["Cylinder A Retracts Step 6"] CyAminus2 --> LimA0["a0 generated"] LimA0 --> G3Reset["3b RESET Group 3"] LimA0 --> G1Set ``` **Power Circuit (text representation):** Each $nS_m$ (e.g., $1S_1$, $1S_2$) is a **5/2 way double-solenoid piloted DCV**. The solenoids are energized by the pilot signals derived above. The DCV routes compressed air to the **cap end** ($A^+$) or **rod end** ($A^-$) of cylinder A, and similarly for cylinder B. --- ### Question B — Design Using Mapping Method (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** > Design a sequential circuit for the application **A+ B+ B− A−** using the **Mapping Method**. Draw the mapping table, write the Boolean equations, and draw the circuit realization using AND/OR elements. **(a)** Draw the **mapping table** and write all **Boolean equations** for each cylinder direction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Show the **AND/OR circuit realization** and explain how the sequence is executed. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Mapping Table & Boolean Equations** | Step | A+ | B+ | B− | A− | Limit Signal Used | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | $a_0$ (start) | | 2 | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 | $a_1$ | | 3 | 0 | 0 | **1** | 0 | $b_1$ | | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | $b_0$ | | 5 (cycle reset) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $a_0$ again | **Boolean Equations (Unsigned):** For each direction, the equation is the **OR** over all steps where the direction is active, with the previous step's end signal as the enabling condition: $$A^{+} = a_0 \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot \text{START}$$ $$B^{+} = a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1}$$ $$B^{-} = b_1 \cdot \overline{b_0}$$ $$A^{-} = b_0 \cdot \overline{a_0'}$$ where $a_0'$ is the limit signal of cylinder A retracted (next cycle). **Simplification using K-Map:** Since each direction appears in **exactly one step**, the equations are already in **minimal Sum-of-Products (SOP)** form. No K-map simplification needed. **Part (b) — Circuit Realization** ```mermaid flowchart TD StartBtn["START a0"] --> AND1["AND Gate Input 1"] LimA1["NOT a1"] --> AND1In2["AND Gate Input 2"] AND1 --> SolAplus["Solenoid 1S1 A+ DCV"] SolAplus --> CyAplus["Cylinder A Extends"] CyAplus --> LimA1b["Limit a1 generated"] LimA1b --> AND2["AND Gate for B+"] LimB1["NOT b1"] --> AND2In2["AND Gate Input 2"] AND2 --> SolBplus["Solenoid 1S2 B+ DCV"] SolBplus --> CyBplus["Cylinder B Extends"] CyBplus --> LimB1b["Limit b1 generated"] LimB1b --> AND3["AND Gate for B-"] LimB0["NOT b0"] --> AND3In2["AND Gate Input 2"] AND3 --> SolBminus["Solenoid 2S1 B- DCV"] SolBminus --> CyBminus["Cylinder B Retracts"] CyBminus --> LimB0b["Limit b0 generated"] LimB0b --> AND4["AND Gate for A-"] LimA0next["NOT a0_next"] --> AND4In2["AND Gate Input 2"] AND4 --> SolAminus["Solenoid 2S2 A- DCV"] SolAminus --> CyAminus["Cylinder A Retracts"] CyAminus --> CycleEnd["Cycle Complete"] CycleEnd --> StartBtn ``` **Pneumatic Realization:** Each AND gate is implemented using a **dual-pressure valve (3/2 way)** that requires **two simultaneous pilot signals** to pass air. The "NOT" function is implemented using a **3/2 way NC valve** (normally closed; opens only when the limit is hit). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2-3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Forgetting the "NOT" condition** in mapping equations. Without $\overline{a_{i+1}}$, the cylinder will **stay extended** into the next step, causing a sequence error. > 2. **Miscounting groups** in cascade method. Always state the **number of groups** and the **end signal of each group** explicitly. > 3. **Drawing only the logic circuit** and forgetting the **power circuit** (cylinder + DCV). Both are required for full marks. > 4. **Wrong assignment of step numbers to groups** in cascade. Group 1 is always the **first** set of steps, and its end signal comes from the **last step of that group**. > 5. **Missing the cycle reset** mechanism. Every sequential circuit must show how it **returns to Step 1** — usually via the last step's end signal feeding back to the start. > 6. **Confusing limit signals** $a_0$ (A retracted) vs $a_1$ (A extended). The KTU convention is: $a_1$ = extended, $a_0$ = retracted. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam prep):** - [x] **Sequential Circuit** = sequence of actuator motions with **memory** of past states. - [x] **Cascade Method** = uses **pneumatic memory pairs** (two 3/2 valves per group). Limited to ~6–8 steps. - [x] **Mapping Method** = uses **Boolean equations** derived from a **mapping table** with "1"s in active cells. - [x] **Step Counter Method** = uses **binary counter + decoder** to track step number; electrically powered. - [x] **Number of Cascade Memory Valves** $M = 2 \times G$ where $G$ = number of groups. - [x] **Number of Groups** $G = \lceil n / k \rceil$ where $k$ = cylinders per group. - [x] **Maximum steps in Step Counter** = $2^n$ where $n$ = counter bits. - [x] **Mapping Equation Form:** $X^+ = \sum (a_i \cdot \overline{a_{i+1}})$. - [x] **Limit signal convention:** $a_1$ = cylinder A extended, $a_0$ = cylinder A retracted. - [x] **Cascade end signal rule:** End of group = limit valve of the **last step** in that group. - [x] **Cycle restart:** Always comes from the **last step's end signal** feeding back to **Group 1 SET**. - [x] **K-map simplification** is essential in mapping method for sequences with **repeated motions** (e.g., B+ appearing twice). - [x] **Decoder IC 74LS138** is a 3-to-8 line decoder commonly used in step counter circuits. - [x] **Cascade method is purely pneumatic** — no electricity required (good for explosive environments). - [x] **Step counter method is the most scalable** — preferred in modern PLC-based systems. - [x] **Mapping method is the algebraic middle ground** — eliminates hardware but requires Boolean analysis. - [x] **KTU 2024 exam pattern:** Cascade = 14 marks, Mapping = 14 marks, or Step Counter = 14 marks (only one of the three is asked, with internal choice). - [x] **Pitfall:** Never draw a sequential circuit without showing the **power circuit + control circuit** both. - [x] **Verification step:** Always create a **truth table** showing group/direction status for each step to confirm the design. ---

topics.title: Compound Circuit Design - Combination Circuit Design - Fault Finding of Circuits - Use of Microprocessors for Sequencing - Introduction to Different Types of Industrial Controllers - Types of Automation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET831 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compound Circuit Design - Combination Circuit Design - Fault Finding of Circuits - Use of Microprocessors for Sequencing - Introduction to Different Types of Industrial Controllers - Types of Automation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Compound Circuit Design

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Compound Hydraulic Circuit** is a circuit in which two or more hydraulic actuators (cylinders or motors) operate simultaneously or in a coordinated sequence, sharing a single power source (pump) but with their own individual flow and pressure control elements. The design ensures that motion of one actuator does not unduly affect the motion of another, and that the system response time, sequencing, and load-sharing characteristics are deterministic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Compound circuits are NOT merely parallel circuits. They involve *combined series-parallel* configurations of valves, accumulators, pressure compensators, and flow dividers that allow coordinated multi-actuator motion from a single pump.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **kitchen sink with two taps** connected to one main water line (the pump). One tap is for the kitchen basin, the other is for the washing machine. Even if both taps are opened at the same time, each delivers a controlled, predictable flow because each has its own valve (flow control). The pressure at the pump might drop slightly, but the *relative* flow to each tap is maintained. A compound hydraulic circuit behaves exactly like this — a single pump serves multiple actuators, but each actuator has its own "tap" (flow/pressure regulator) that makes its motion independent of the others.

---

## 1.2 Combination Circuit Design

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Combination Circuit** (also called a *composite* or *regenerative* circuit) is a special class of hydraulic circuit where two or more basic circuit types (series, parallel, regenerative, counterbalance, meter-out, meter-in) are combined to produce a specific desired output. Combination circuits are used to achieve **fast approach + slow working + fast return** cycles, or to recycle flow for energy savings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** A combination circuit is designed by *superimposing* the function of one basic circuit onto another, with the goal of optimizing cycle time, energy efficiency, and load handling.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **car's automatic transmission**. It combines the principles of a manual gear system (series/parallel torque paths), a hydraulic torque converter (fluid coupling), and an electronic controller. Each subsystem by itself is simple, but the *combination* produces smooth, efficient motion across speeds. A combination hydraulic circuit is the hydraulic equivalent — basic building blocks are intelligently merged.

---

## 1.3 Fault Finding (Troubleshooting) of Hydraulic Circuits

### Formal KTU Definition
**Fault finding** is the systematic process of identifying, locating, and rectifying malfunctions in a hydraulic/pneumatic system using logical diagnostic procedures, instrumentation (pressure gauges, flow meters, thermocouples), and visual/auditory inspection. It employs the **"5-Step Fault Diagnosis Methodology"**: Symptom → Cause → Test → Confirm → Rectify.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** Approximately **80% of all hydraulic system failures** are caused by: (1) contaminated fluid, (2) leakage, (3) incorrect pressure setting, and (4) air ingress. This 80/20 rule is a guaranteed high-yield topic.

### Conceptual Analogy
A hydraulic fault-finding exercise is like a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. The patient reports symptoms (machine runs slow), the doctor forms hypotheses (low fluid, blocked filter, worn pump), orders tests (pressure gauge reading, fluid sampling), confirms the diagnosis, and prescribes treatment (change filter, replace seals). The process is identical — **systematic, instrument-based, and hypothesis-driven**.

---

## 1.4 Use of Microprocessors for Sequencing

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Microprocessor-Based Sequencer** replaces the conventional cam-operated drum timer or relay-based sequencer in a hydraulic circuit with a programmable electronic controller. The microprocessor executes a stored program (ladder logic or Boolean logic) that energizes solenoid-operated directional control valves (DCVs) in a predetermined time-based or event-based sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The modern industrial sequencing standard is the **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — a ruggedized microprocessor designed specifically for harsh industrial environments (vibration, dust, EMI, temperature extremes of **0 °C to 60 °C**).

### Conceptual Analogy
A microprocessor sequencer is like a **digital alarm clock with multiple snooze buttons**. Each "snooze" event triggers a different action (turn on radio, brew coffee, open blinds). The clock "remembers" the order and timing. The PLC "remembers" the sequence of valve actuations and executes them reliably, millions of times, without fatigue or error.

---

## 1.5 Introduction to Different Types of Industrial Controllers

### Formal KTU Definition
An **Industrial Controller** is a device that receives input signals from sensors (pressure, flow, temperature, position, limit switches), processes them according to a control algorithm (ON-OFF, PID, fuzzy, adaptive), and generates output commands to final control elements (solenoid valves, motors, heaters). Industrial controllers are classified as: **PLC, DCS, SCADA, RTU, CNC, PID Controller, Microcontroller-based Controller**, and **PC-based Soft-PLC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** The three industrial control architectures that *must* be known are: (1) **PLC** (machine-level), (2) **DCS** (process-level), (3) **SCADA** (supervisory-level). Students frequently confuse these in exams — the difference is the **scope** of control.

### Conceptual Analogy
- **PLC** is like the **brain of a single machine** (e.g., one CNC lathe).
- **DCS** is like the **brain of an entire factory floor** (e.g., an oil refinery with 500 pumps and 200 reactors).
- **SCADA** is like the **remote eye** that watches a city-wide water distribution network from a central control room.

---

## 1.6 Types of Automation System

### Formal KTU Definition
**Industrial Automation** is the use of control systems (mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electronic, and computer-based) to operate industrial processes with minimal human intervention. The three classical types are: **Fixed (Hard) Automation**, **Programmable Automation**, and **Flexible (Soft) Automation**. A fourth modern category, **Integrated (Intelligent) Automation**, is now standard in Industry 4.0.

| Type | Key Feature | Volume | Variety | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Automation** | Dedicated sequence | Very High | Very Low | Car engine assembly line |
| **Programmable Automation** | Batch production | Medium | Medium | CNC machining center |
| **Flexible Automation** | On-the-fly changeover | Low–Medium | High | FMS, robotic welding cell |
| **Integrated Automation** | IoT + AI + CPS | Variable | Very High | Smart factory (Industry 4.0) |

### Conceptual Analogy
- **Fixed Automation** is like a **dedicated pasta-making machine** in a factory — it only makes spaghetti, but it makes millions per hour.
- **Programmable Automation** is like a **3D printer** — you write a new program each time for a new shape.
- **Flexible Automation** is like a **modern robotic kitchen** that can switch from making sushi to pizza on demand.
- **Integrated Automation** is like a **smart home** where the fridge, oven, lights, and AC all "talk" to each other and adjust automatically.

---

## 1.7 Module 4 Big-Picture Concept Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How These Topics Interconnect:**
> 1. Compound/Combination circuits are the *physical layer* of automation.
> 2. Microprocessors/PLCs are the *logic layer* that sequences these circuits.
> 3. Industrial Controllers are the *generalized hardware category* of these logic devices.
> 4. Fault Finding is the *maintenance layer* that keeps the system alive.
> 5. Types of Automation is the *system-level framework* within which all of the above exist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Compound Circuit — Working Principles in Depth

A compound circuit typically contains:
- **One pump** (fixed or variable displacement)
- **One reservoir**
- **Two or more actuators** (cylinders / motors)
- **One DCV per actuator** (typically 4/3 solenoid-operated)
- **One flow control valve per actuator** (for speed regulation)
- **One pressure relief valve** (system safety)
- **Optional:** pressure-compensated flow divider, sequence valve, counterbalance valve

### 2.1.1 The Core Operating Principle
The pump delivers a flow $Q_P$ that is split among the $n$ actuators. The instantaneous flow to actuator $i$ is:

$$
Q_i \;=\; C_i \cdot A_i \cdot v_i
$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = discharge coefficient (typically **0.95–0.98** for sharp-edged orifices)
- $A_i$ = effective piston area ($m^2$)
- $v_i$ = piston velocity ($m/s$)

The pump pressure $P_P$ must overcome the highest load pressure plus all line losses:

$$
P_P \;=\; \max(P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n) \;+\; \sum \Delta P_{\text{lines}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The pump pressure is determined by the *highest-load* actuator, not by the average. This is why compound circuits often waste energy — unloaded actuators still see full system pressure.

### 2.1.2 Flow Divider (Pressure Compensated)
To ensure *exact* flow split regardless of load, a **pressure-compensated flow divider** is used. It maintains a constant pressure drop $\Delta P_{\text{comp}}$ across a variable orifice.

$$
Q_{\text{out},i} \;=\; K_i \cdot A_{\text{orifice},i} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P_{\text{comp}}}{\rho}}
$$

Where $\rho$ is the fluid density (**870 kg/m³ for mineral oil at 40 °C**).

---

## 2.2 Combination Circuit — Regenerative and Fast-Cycle Designs

### 2.2.1 Regenerative Circuit (Energy Recovery)
In a regenerative circuit, the return flow from the rod-end of a double-acting cylinder is *added* to the pump flow entering the cap-end, doubling the extension speed at reduced force. The condition is:

$$
Q_{\text{cap}} \;=\; Q_{\text{pump}} \;+\; Q_{\text{rod}}
$$

For a cylinder with area ratio $\beta = A_{\text{rod}} / A_{\text{cap}}$:

$$
v_{\text{regen}} \;=\; v_{\text{pump}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 - \beta}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Formula:** The extension force during regeneration reduces to:
> $$ F_{\text{regen}} \;=\; P \cdot (A_{\text{cap}} - A_{\text{rod}}) \;=\; P \cdot A_{\text{cap}} \cdot (1 - \beta) $$
> This is why regeneration is used only for *no-load or light-load* strokes (fast approach).

### 2.2.2 Meter-In / Meter-Out / Bleed-Off Speed Control
- **Meter-In:** Flow control on the inlet line — controls extension speed, good against *positive* loads.
- **Meter-Out:** Flow control on the return line — controls retraction speed, prevents *cavitation* and controls *negative* loads.
- **Bleed-Off:** Excess flow diverted back to tank — used with single-rod cylinders only.

---

## 2.3 Fault Finding — The 5-Step Diagnostic Methodology

| Step | Action | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Observe Symptom** | Slow motion, overheating, noise, leakage | Visual, auditory, thermal camera |
| 2. **Hypothesize Cause** | Map symptom to fault tree | Knowledge base, FMEA chart |
| 3. **Test with Instruments** | Measure P, Q, T at key points | Pressure gauge, flow meter, thermocouple, tachometer |
| 4. **Confirm Hypothesis** | Isolate component, bench test | Test bench, multimeter |
| 5. **Rectify** | Repair / replace / recalibrate | Service kit, calibration tool |

### 2.3.1 The Standard Hydraulic Fault Triangle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Parameter Triangle:** Pressure, Flow, Temperature. If *any one* of these deviates from spec, the other two will too. Always start fault diagnosis by measuring these three at the pump outlet.

$$
\Delta P_{\text{system}} \;=\; P_{\text{pump}} - P_{\text{return}}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{volumetric}} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\% \quad (\text{normal} > 90\%)
$$

---

## 2.4 Microprocessor-Based Sequencing — Architecture

### 2.4.1 Generic PLC Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```
[Sensors / Limit Switches] → [INPUT MODULE] → [CPU + Memory] → [OUTPUT MODULE] → [Solenoid Coils]
```

### 2.4.2 Scan Cycle Time
The PLC executes a repetitive **scan cycle**:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{program}} + T_{\text{output}} + T_{\text{housekeeping}}
$$

Typical scan times are **5 ms to 50 ms** for small PLCs and **0.5 ms to 5 ms** for high-end PLCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For sequencing hydraulic cylinders, scan time must be **< 10%** of the shortest cylinder stroke time. Otherwise the sequence becomes unreliable due to missed inputs.

### 2.4.3 Boolean Logic for Sequencing
Sequencing logic is expressed in **Boolean algebra**. The three fundamental operations:

$$
\text{AND} \;=\; A \cdot B \quad (\text{both inputs ON})
$$

$$
\text{OR} \;=\; A + B \quad (\text{either input ON})
$$

$$
\text{NOT} \;=\; \overline{A} \quad (\text{inverted input})
$$

A sequence like *"Extend Cylinder A only when Limit Switch 1 is ON AND Emergency Stop is OFF"* is written as:

$$
Y_{\text{solenoid}} \;=\; LS_1 \cdot \overline{E\text{-}STOP}
$$

---

## 2.5 Industrial Controllers — Comparative Analysis

### 2.5.1 PLC vs DCS vs SCADA

| Feature | PLC | DCS | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single machine | Whole plant | Multi-site |
| **I/O Count** | 16 – 4096 | 1000 – 100,000 | 10,000 – 1,000,000+ |
| **Response Time** | ms | 100 ms – 1 s | seconds |
| **Redundancy** | Optional | Built-in | Distributed |
| **Programming** | Ladder, FBD | Function blocks, SFC | Tags, scripts |
| **Example Brand** | Siemens S7-1200 | Honeywell Experion | Wonderware InTouch |

### 2.5.2 PID Controller (Continuous Process Control)

The PID output $u(t)$ is given by:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_p \cdot e(t) \;+\; K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $K_i$ = integral gain (1/s)
- $K_d$ = derivative gain (s)
- $e(t)$ = error = setpoint − measured value

The discrete (PLC-implementable) form is:

$$
u_k \;=\; K_p e_k \;+\; K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j \;+\; \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

Where $T_s$ is the sampling time.

---

## 2.6 Types of Automation — Detailed Comparison

### 2.6.1 Fixed Automation (Hard Automation)
- High initial investment, very low per-unit cost at high volume
- Cycle time **< 5 seconds** typical
- Used in automotive, bottling, electronics assembly
- Re-tooling cost is prohibitive

### 2.6.2 Programmable Automation
- Production batch size: **50 – 10,000 units**
- Changeover time: **minutes to hours**
- CNC machines, batch chemical reactors

### 2.6.3 Flexible Automation
- **Random mix** of products in any order
- Changeover time: **seconds** (no physical retooling)
- FMS (Flexible Manufacturing System), robotic cells

### 2.6.4 Integrated / Intelligent Automation (Industry 4.0)
- Combines **IoT + AI + Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) + Cloud + Big Data**
- Self-optimizing, self-diagnosing, self-configuring
- Example: Siemens Amberg factory — **75% automated**, produces 17 million SIMATIC units/year

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table contains the *only* formulas you need to memorize for Module 4 numerical problems. All symbols follow ISO 1219 / ISO 5599 conventions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_i = C_d A_i v_i$ | Flow to actuator $i$ | $m^3/s$ | Compound circuits |
| 2 | $P_P = \max(P_i) + \Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ | Pump pressure | Pa | All multi-actuator circuits |
| 3 | $v_{\text{regen}} = v_{\text{pump}} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\beta}$ | Regen velocity | m/s | $\beta < 1$ |
| 4 | $F_{\text{regen}} = P \cdot A_{\text{cap}}(1-\beta)$ | Regen force | N | Light load only |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = Q_{\text{act}}/Q_{\text{theor}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency | % | Pump health check |
| 6 | $T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{house}}$ | PLC scan time | ms | Sequencing design |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID output | V / mA | Process control |
| 8 | $A_{\text{cap}} - A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | Net cylinder area | $m^2$ | All cylinder calcs |
| 9 | $P_{\text{hyd}} = P \cdot Q / 600$ | Hydraulic power | kW | Pump sizing |
| 10 | $LS_1 \cdot \overline{E\text{-}STOP}$ | Sequencing AND-NOT | Boolean | Ladder logic |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Compound circuits drive 4 robotic axes simultaneously from one pump.
- **Injection Molding Machines:** Combination circuits provide fast-close, slow-injection, fast-open.
- **Aircraft Flight Control:** Triple-redundant PLCs for safety-critical sequencing.
- **Oil Refineries:** DCS systems manage **> 50,000 I/O points** with millisecond response.
- **Smart Factories:** Siemens Amberg, Bosch Blaichach, GE Brilliant Factory — all use integrated automation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Cylinder Velocity in a Compound Circuit with Flow Divider

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A hydraulic system has one pump delivering $Q_P = 60$ L/min to two cylinders (Cyl-A and Cyl-B) via a **50:50 flow divider**. Cyl-A has bore $D_A = 80$ mm, Cyl-B has bore $D_B = 50$ mm. Both have the same rod diameter $d = 40$ mm. Find the velocity of each cylinder's piston during extension.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert the pump flow to SI units.**

$$
Q_P \;=\; 60 \,\frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}} \;\times\; \frac{1\,m^3}{1000\,L} \;\times\; \frac{1\,\text{min}}{60\,s} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \,\frac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the flow split through the divider.**

For a 50:50 flow divider, each actuator receives half the pump flow:

$$
Q_A \;=\; Q_B \;=\; \frac{Q_P}{2} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \,\frac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the cap-end area of Cyl-A.**

$$
A_{\text{cap},A} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D_A^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2
$$

Evaluating the numerical value:

$$
A_{\text{cap},A} \;=\; 0.7854 \times 0.0064 \;=\; 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \,m^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute the velocity of Cyl-A's piston.**

Using the continuity equation $Q = A \cdot v$, we rearrange for velocity:

$$
v_A \;=\; \frac{Q_A}{A_{\text{cap},A}}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
v_A \;=\; \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Evaluating the quotient:

$$
v_A \;=\; 9.946 \times 10^{-2} \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 0.0995 \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 5.97 \,\frac{m}{\text{min}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the cap-end area of Cyl-B.**

$$
A_{\text{cap},B} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D_B^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2
$$

Evaluating the numerical value:

$$
A_{\text{cap},B} \;=\; 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \;=\; 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \,m^2
$$

**Step 6: Compute the velocity of Cyl-B's piston.**

$$
v_B \;=\; \frac{Q_B}{A_{\text{cap},B}} \;=\; \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Evaluating the quotient:

$$
v_B \;=\; 2.547 \times 10^{-1} \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 0.255 \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 15.28 \,\frac{m}{\text{min}}
$$

**Step 7: Interpretation.**

Cyl-B moves **2.56 times faster** than Cyl-A because of its smaller bore area. This confirms the rule: *in a compound circuit with equal flow split, smaller cylinders move faster.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Regenerative Circuit Speed Gain

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A double-acting cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm and rod $d = 60$ mm. In a normal (non-regenerative) extension, the pump delivers $Q_P = 40$ L/min to the cap end. Calculate the extension velocity in (a) normal mode, and (b) regenerative mode.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the area ratio $\beta$.**

The rod area is:

$$
A_{\text{rod}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (0.060)^2 \;=\; 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \,m^2
$$

The cap area is:

$$
A_{\text{cap}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \,m^2
$$

Therefore the area ratio is:

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{rod}}}{A_{\text{cap}}} \;=\; \frac{2.827 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Evaluating the quotient:

$$
\beta \;=\; 0.360
$$

**Step 2: Compute the normal-mode velocity.**

Convert the pump flow:

$$
Q_P \;=\; 40 \,\frac{L}{\min} \;=\; 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \,\frac{m^3}{s}
$$

Normal-mode velocity (flow only into cap end):

$$
v_{\text{normal}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P}{A_{\text{cap}}} \;=\; \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
v_{\text{normal}} \;=\; 8.488 \times 10^{-2} \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 0.085 \,\frac{m}{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the regenerative-mode velocity.**

Using the regenerative velocity formula:

$$
v_{\text{regen}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P}{A_{\text{cap}} - A_{\text{rod}}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P}{A_{\text{cap}}(1 - \beta)}
$$

Compute the effective area first:

$$
A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; A_{\text{cap}}(1 - \beta) \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.360) \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.640
$$

Evaluating:

$$
A_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \,m^2
$$

Now compute the regenerative velocity:

$$
v_{\text{regen}} \;=\; \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
v_{\text{regen}} \;=\; 1.326 \times 10^{-1} \,\frac{m}{s} \;\approx\; 0.133 \,\frac{m}{s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the speed gain ratio.**

$$
\text{Speed Gain} \;=\; \frac{v_{\text{regen}}}{v_{\text{normal}}} \;=\; \frac{0.133}{0.085}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
\text{Speed Gain} \;=\; 1.563
$$

Or equivalently using the formula:

$$
\text{Speed Gain} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \beta} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - 0.360} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.640} \;=\; 1.5625
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The two methods agree to within rounding. ✓

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

The regenerative mode is **56% faster** than normal mode but produces **36% less force** (because the effective area is 64% of the cap area). This trade-off is why regeneration is used only for *unloaded fast approach* strokes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PLC Scan Cycle Timing for Sequencing

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
A PLC controls a 4-cylinder hydraulic press with the shortest stroke time $T_{\text{stroke,min}} = 1.2$ s. The PLC has: input scan time $T_{\text{in}} = 3$ ms, program execution $T_{\text{prog}} = 8$ ms, output scan $T_{\text{out}} = 2$ ms, and housekeeping $T_{\text{house}} = 1$ ms. Verify whether the scan time meets the 10% rule.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the total scan time.**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; T_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{prog}} + T_{\text{out}} + T_{\text{house}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; 3 + 8 + 2 + 1
$$

Evaluating:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; 14 \,ms
$$

**Step 2: Compute the 10% threshold of the minimum stroke time.**

$$
T_{\text{limit}} \;=\; 0.10 \times T_{\text{stroke,min}}
$$

Substitute the value:

$$
T_{\text{limit}} \;=\; 0.10 \times 1.2 \,s \;=\; 0.12 \,s \;=\; 120 \,ms
$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \;=\; 14 \,ms \;<\; 120 \,ms \;=\; T_{\text{limit}}
$$

The condition is satisfied. The scan time is **only 11.7%** of the limit — well within safe operating margin.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PID Output for a Hydraulic Pressure Loop

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup
A hydraulic pressure regulator uses a PID controller. Setpoint $SP = 100$ bar, measured $PV = 92$ bar, previous error $e_{k-1} = 6$ bar, sampling time $T_s = 0.1$ s, gains $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 0.5 \,s^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.05$ s. Sum of past errors $= 50$ bar·s. Compute the controller output $u_k$.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the current error.**

$$
e_k \;=\; SP - PV \;=\; 100 - 92 \;=\; 8 \,bar
$$

**Step 2: Compute the proportional term.**

$$
P \;=\; K_p \cdot e_k \;=\; 2 \times 8 \;=\; 16
$$

**Step 3: Compute the integral term.**

$$
I \;=\; K_i \cdot T_s \cdot \sum e_j \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.1 \times 50 \;=\; 2.5
$$

**Step 4: Compute the derivative term.**

$$
D \;=\; \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_k - e_{k-1}) \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.1}(8 - 6) \;=\; 0.5 \times 2 \;=\; 1.0
$$

**Step 5: Sum to get the controller output.**

$$
u_k \;=\; P + I + D \;=\; 16 + 2.5 + 1.0 \;=\; 19.5
$$

The output (typically scaled to 4–20 mA or 0–10 V) drives the proportional valve.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix:** The table below is a *guaranteed KTU exam high-yield table*. Memorize the conditions.

| Situation | Use This Circuit / Controller |
|---|---|
| Two cylinders, equal speed demand | Compound + flow divider |
| Fast approach + slow work stroke | Combination (regen + meter-out) |
| Heavy negative load on vertical cylinder | Counterbalance valve + meter-out |
| Need ON/OFF sequencing of 4 valves | PLC with ladder logic |
| Need continuous pressure/temperature control | PID controller / DCS |
| Remote monitoring of 50+ machines | SCADA with RTUs |
| High-volume dedicated production | Fixed automation |
| Batch production of 100s of variants | Programmable automation (CNC) |
| Mixed-model production | Flexible automation (FMS) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Compound Hydraulic Circuit — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Reservoir Tank] --> node2[Suction Strainer]
    node2 --> node3[Fixed Displacement Pump]
    node3 --> node4[Pressure Relief Valve 200 bar set]
    node3 --> node5[Pressure Compensated Flow Divider 50:50]
    node5 --> node6[Flow Control Valve FCV A]
    node5 --> node7[Flow Control Valve FCV B]
    node6 --> node8[DCV 4 by 3 Solenoid Cyl A]
    node7 --> node9[DCV 4 by 3 Solenoid Cyl B]
    node8 --> node10[Double Acting Cylinder A 80mm bore]
    node9 --> node11[Double Acting Cylinder B 50mm bore]
    node8 --> node12[Tank Return Line]
    node9 --> node12
    node4 --> node12
```

**Reading the diagram:** Fluid travels from the tank (node1) → pump (node3) → relief valve (node4, for safety) → flow divider (node5) → individual flow control valves (node6, node7) → individual DCVs (node8, node9) → individual cylinders (node10, node11) → return to tank (node12). Each cylinder has independent speed control.

---

## 4.2 Combination Circuit — Regenerative + Meter-Out (Fast Approach, Slow Return)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP[Pump 40 L per min] --> nodePRV[Pressure Relief Valve]
    nodeP --> nodeDCV[DCV 4 by 3 Position A and B and C]
    nodeDCV -- Position A Regeneration --> nodeREG[Regen Check Valve]
    nodeDCV -- Position B Normal Extend --> nodeMC[Metre Out FCV]
    nodeDCV -- Position C Retract --> nodeRT[Tank Return]
    nodeREG --> nodeCYL[Double Acting Cylinder 100 by 60]
    nodeMC --> nodeCYL
    nodeRT --> nodeCYL
    nodeCYL --> nodeDCV
```

**Reading the diagram:** In **Position A**, the rod-end flow is recirculated to the cap-end (regeneration → fast approach). In **Position B**, flow goes through the meter-out valve (slow working stroke). In **Position C**, flow returns freely to tank (fast retract).

---

## 4.3 PLC Architecture for Hydraulic Sequencing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[Limit Switch 1 LS1] --> nodeIM
    nodeS2[Pressure Switch PS1] --> nodeIM
    nodeS3[Push Button PB Start] --> nodeIM
    nodeS4[Push Button PB Stop] --> nodeIM
    nodeIM[Input Module 24V DC Isolated] --> nodeCPU
    nodeMEM[Memory RAM plus EEPROM Ladder Program] --> nodeCPU
    nodeCPU[CPU Microprocessor Scan Cycle] --> nodeOM
    nodePWR[Power Supply 24V DC] --> nodeIM
    nodePWR --> nodeCPU
    nodePWR --> nodeOM
    nodeOM[Output Module Relay plus Transistor] --> nodeV1[Solenoid Coil Y1 Cylinder A Extend]
    nodeOM --> nodeV2[Solenoid Coil Y2 Cylinder A Retract]
    nodeOM --> nodeV3[Solenoid Coil Y3 Cylinder B Extend]
    nodeOM --> nodeV4[Solenoid Coil Y4 Cylinder B Retract]
    nodeHMI[Human Machine Interface HMI Touchscreen] --> nodeCPU
    nodeCPU --> nodeHMI
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inputs (limit switches, pressure switches, push buttons) enter the PLC through the input module. The CPU executes the ladder program (stored in memory). Outputs drive the four solenoid coils that actuate the hydraulic DCVs. The HMI provides the operator interface.

---

## 4.4 Industrial Automation Hierarchy — Purdue Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL5[Level 5 Enterprise Network ERP SAP]
    nodeL4[Level 4 Site Manufacturing Operations MOM]
    nodeL3[Level 3 Supervisory SCADA HMI Historian]
    nodeL2[Level 2 Control DCS PLC PID]
    nodeL1[Level 1 Basic Actuators Sensors Motors Valves]
    nodeL0[Level 0 Physical Process]
    nodeL5 --> nodeL4 --> nodeL3 --> nodeL2 --> nodeL1 --> nodeL0
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** — the globally accepted 5-level hierarchy of industrial automation. Hydraulic components typically operate at **Level 0–1**, PLCs at **Level 2**, SCADA at **Level 3**, and ERP at **Level 5**.

---

## 4.5 Fault-Finding Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Machine Symptom Reported] --> nodeB{Which symptom category}
    nodeB -- Slow motion --> nodeC[Check pump flow and pressure]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Q less than 90 percent rated}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Replace pump or repair wear plates]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[Check for internal leakage in cylinders]
    nodeB -- Overheating --> nodeG[Tank temperature above 60 deg C]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Check cooler and relief valve setting]
    nodeB -- Noise cavitation --> nodeI[Check fluid level and air ingress]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Check suction strainer and line seals]
    nodeB -- Erratic motion --> nodeK[Check air in system and contaminated fluid]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Flush system and bleed air]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start from the symptom. Each branch leads to a category, then to a measurement, then to a specific component to inspect. This is the **hypothesis-driven diagnostic flowchart** used in industry.

---

## 4.6 Automation Type Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[New Production Requirement] --> nodeB{Annual Volume}
    nodeB -- Greater than 1 million units --> nodeC{Variety}
    nodeC -- Low 1 to 3 SKUs --> nodeD[Fixed Hard Automation]
    nodeC -- High more than 10 SKUs --> nodeE[Flexible Automation FMS]
    nodeB -- 10000 to 1 million units --> nodeF[Programmable Automation CNC]
    nodeB -- Less than 10000 units --> nodeG[Manual plus Assist or Robot Cell]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Industry 4.0 Layer Optional]
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

**Reading the diagram:** Select the automation type based on **annual volume × product variety**. Industry 4.0 (IoT/AI) can be added to any of the four classical types.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a compound hydraulic circuit. How does it differ from a simple parallel circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **compound hydraulic circuit** is one in which two or more actuators operate from a single pump but have *individual flow and pressure control elements*, enabling coordinated yet independent motion. A simple parallel circuit, by contrast, has multiple actuators connected in parallel with no individual flow regulation — leading to unpredictable motion when more than one actuator is activated.

| Feature | Compound Circuit | Simple Parallel Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Flow Control | Individual FCV per actuator | None |
| Speed Regulation | Independent | Unpredictable |
| Energy Efficiency | Higher (uses flow divider) | Lower |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Use Case | Multi-axis machines | Rare, educational only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Comparison table (1.5 marks).

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: List the three classical types of industrial automation. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three classical types are:

1. **Fixed (Hard) Automation** — Used for high-volume, low-variety production. *Example:* Automotive engine assembly line (produces one engine variant, 1,500 units/day).

2. **Programmable Automation** — Used for medium-volume, medium-variety batch production. *Example:* CNC machining center (program changed per batch).

3. **Flexible (Soft) Automation** — Used for low-to-medium volume, high-variety mixed production. *Example:* Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) with robotic cells that switch between part types in minutes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Naming (1.5 marks) + Examples (1.5 marks).

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B must be answered with internal choice. Both **Question A** and **Question B** carry 14 marks and are based on Module 4. You must attempt *only one*.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a regenerative circuit in a hydraulic system. Derive the expression for the velocity ratio between regenerative and normal extension.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
In a regenerative circuit, during the *extension* stroke, the DCV directs the pump flow to the cap end of the cylinder. The rod-end discharge, instead of going to the tank, is *added back* to the pump flow via a regeneration check valve. This doubles the effective flow into the cap end, doubling the extension velocity. The trade-off is a reduced extension force.

**Skeletal Sketch (1.5 marks):**

```
[ Pump ] → [ DCV Position A ] → Cap end [ Cylinder ] 
                                     ↑
                                     └─ Rod end (regen check valve) → joins pump line
```

**Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Let $\beta = A_{\text{rod}}/A_{\text{cap}}$. The effective flow entering the cap end in regenerative mode is:

$$
Q_{\text{eff}} \;=\; Q_P + Q_{\text{rod}} \;=\; Q_P + Q_P \cdot \beta \;=\; Q_P (1 + \beta)
$$

The regenerative velocity is therefore:

$$
v_{\text{regen}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P (1 + \beta)}{A_{\text{cap}}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P (1 + \beta)}{A_{\text{cap}}}
$$

The normal velocity is:

$$
v_{\text{normal}} \;=\; \frac{Q_P}{A_{\text{cap}}}
$$

Taking the ratio:

$$
\frac{v_{\text{regen}}}{v_{\text{normal}}} \;=\; 1 + \beta
$$

Therefore the **velocity ratio** is $1 + \beta$. For $\beta = 0.36$ (typical), the speed gain is **1.36×**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Derivation (more common in textbooks):** Some texts derive the ratio as $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$, where the pump flow is instead redirected to the *rod* end during regen. Always check the convention in the question.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the fault-finding methodology for a hydraulic system that exhibits "slow cylinder extension". List the probable causes and the instruments used to diagnose them.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Symptom:** Cylinder extends slower than rated specification.

**Step-by-Step Methodology (3 marks):**

1. **Verify the symptom:** Use a stopwatch to time the stroke against rated spec.
2. **Measure pump outlet pressure** with a calibrated pressure gauge. Compare to nameplate value.
3. **Measure pump flow** with a flow meter (turbine or gear type). Compare to rated $Q_P$.
4. **Compute volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = Q_{\text{actual}}/Q_{\text{theoretical}} \times 100$. If $< 85\%$, pump is worn.
5. **Check relief valve setting:** A relief set too low diverts pump flow back to tank.
6. **Check internal leakage in cylinder:** Conduct a cylinder drift test (dead-stroke test).
7. **Check fluid viscosity:** Cold fluid increases leakage and reduces flow.

**Probable Causes (2 marks):**

| Rank | Cause | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pump wear (low volumetric efficiency) | 35% |
| 2 | Internal leakage in cylinder (bypass across piston seal) | 25% |
| 3 | Incorrect relief valve setting | 15% |
| 4 | Blocked suction strainer (cavitation) | 10% |
| 5 | Low fluid level / air ingress | 8% |
| 6 | Wrong fluid viscosity (wrong ISO VG grade) | 7% |

**Instruments Used (2 marks):**

- Pressure gauge (0–400 bar, ±1% accuracy)
- Flow meter (turbine type, ±2% accuracy)
- Tachometer / stopwatch
- Thermocouple (for fluid temperature)
- Viscosity cup / kinematic viscometer
- Particle counter (for contamination check)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner Note):** Students often jump to "replace the pump" without first checking the relief valve setting or fluid viscosity — both are *zero-cost* fixes. Always follow the 5-step methodology before recommending component replacement. This costs **2–3 marks** in valuation.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the architecture of a PLC. Describe how a PLC sequences the operation of two hydraulic cylinders.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**PLC Architecture (3.5 marks):**

A PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) consists of six major blocks:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Executes the user program. Typical 32-bit microprocessor running at 100 MHz – 1 GHz.
2. **Memory:** ROM/Flash for system firmware; RAM for user program and data; EEPROM for retentive storage.
3. **Input Module:** Receives signals from field sensors (24 V DC digital, 4–20 mA analog). Provides optical isolation (typically **1,500 V RMS**).
4. **Output Module:** Sends commands to actuators (24 V DC transistor, relay, or 4–20 mA analog).
5. **Power Supply:** Converts mains AC (110/230 V) to 24 V DC for internal use.
6. **Programming Device:** HMI or PC with ladder logic editor (e.g., TIA Portal for Siemens).

**Sequencing Two Hydraulic Cylinders (3.5 marks):**

**Sequence:** Extend Cyl-A → Wait for LS1 → Retract Cyl-A → Extend Cyl-B → Wait for LS2 → Retract Cyl-B → Repeat.

**Ladder Logic:**

```
| LS_Start  E_STOP_OK  LS1_OK  |   Y1 (Cyl-A Extend Solenoid)   |
|---||-------||---------||------|---( )-----------------------------|

| LS_Start  E_STOP_OK  LS2_OK  |   Y3 (Cyl-B Extend Solenoid)   |
|---||-------||---------||------|---( )-----------------------------|
```

**Operation:** When the operator presses Start, and Emergency Stop is OFF, and the appropriate limit switch confirms the cylinder is ready, the corresponding solenoid is energized. The PLC's scan cycle (typically 5–20 ms) continuously re-evaluates this logic. When LS1 is hit (Cyl-A fully extended), the program de-energizes Y1 and energizes Y2 (retract). After Cyl-A returns, Y3 is energized, extending Cyl-B.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Architecture diagram (2 marks) + block description (1.5 marks) + ladder logic + sequence explanation (3.5 marks).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare PLC, DCS, and SCADA. State two industrial applications for each.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | PLC | DCS | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Programmable Logic Controller | Distributed Control System | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition |
| **Scope** | Machine-level | Plant-level | Multi-site / utility-level |
| **Typical I/O** | 16 – 4,096 | 1,000 – 100,000 | 10,000 – 1,000,000+ |
| **Response Time** | 1–50 ms | 100 ms – 1 s | seconds |
| **Architecture** | Centralized or rack-based | Distributed (controllers networked) | Central master + remote RTUs |
| **Programming** | Ladder, FBD, STL | Function Blocks, SFC, Continuous Function Chart | Tag-based, scripting |
| **Redundancy** | Optional | Built-in (redundant controllers, I/O, power) | Distributed (failsafe RTUs) |
| **Vendor Examples** | Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix | Honeywell Experion, ABB 800xA, Emerson DeltaV | Wonderware InTouch, GE iFIX, WinCC |

**Applications (3 marks — 1 mark per pair per system, 0.5 each):**

| System | Application 1 | Application 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **PLC** | Automotive welding robot control | Escalator / elevator control |
| **DCS** | Oil refinery (crude distillation unit) | Power plant boiler control |
| **SCADA** | City-wide water distribution network | National electrical grid monitoring |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner Note):** Students often write that SCADA *controls* processes — it does NOT. SCADA *supervises* and *monitors*; the actual control is done by PLCs/RTUs in the field. This conceptual error costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This and Carry to Exam Hall.**

### A. Compound Circuit Design
- Definition: Multiple actuators, single pump, *independent* speed control.
- Flow divider types: **50:50, 60:40, pressure-compensated**.
- Key formula: $Q_i = Q_P \times (\text{split ratio})$.
- Pump pressure follows the *highest-load* actuator.

### B. Combination Circuit Design
- **Regenerative** circuit doubles speed at half force.
- **Meter-out** prevents cavitation on negative loads.
- **Counterbalance** prevents runaway on vertical loads.
- **Sequence valve** enforces order of motion.
- Common combination: *Regen + meter-out + counterbalance* in injection molding.

### C. Fault Finding of Circuits
- The **80/20 rule**: 80% of failures from 4 causes (contamination, leakage, wrong pressure, air).
- The **5-step methodology**: Observe → Hypothesize → Test → Confirm → Rectify.
- The **3-parameter triangle**: Pressure, Flow, Temperature.
- Dead-stroke test for cylinder leakage.

### D. Microprocessors for Sequencing
- **PLC** is a ruggedized microprocessor for industrial sequencing.
- Scan cycle = Input + Program + Output + Housekeeping.
- Scan time must be **< 10%** of shortest cylinder stroke.
- Ladder logic uses Boolean operators: AND (·), OR (+), NOT (overbar).

### E. Industrial Controllers
- **PLC** — single machine, ms response.
- **DCS** — entire plant, redundant, 100 ms response.
- **SCADA** — multi-site monitoring, seconds response.
- **PID** — continuous control: $u = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **RTU** — remote terminal unit, used with SCADA.

### F. Types of Automation
- **Fixed** — high volume, low variety (auto assembly line).
- **Programmable** — medium volume, medium variety (CNC).
- **Flexible** — mixed-model, FMS, robotic cells.
- **Integrated / Industry 4.0** — IoT + AI + CPS + Cloud.
- Selection rule: Match automation type to **annual volume × product variety**.

### G. Cross-Topic Connections (Frequently Asked)
- "How does a PLC sequence a compound circuit?" → Scan → Ladder → Solenoid → DCV → Cylinder.
- "How would you fault-find a PLC-controlled hydraulic press?" → Symptom in cylinder → trace back to PLC input → check I/O → check program.
- "Differentiate flexible and programmable automation" → Changeover time: seconds vs. hours.

### H. Numerical Problems Practice Set
1. Cylinder velocity with flow divider. *(Solution template: see Section 3.1)*
2. Regenerative speed gain. *(Solution template: see Section 3.2)*
3. PLC scan time validation. *(Solution template: see Section 3.3)*
4. PID output calculation. *(Solution template: see Section 3.4)*

### I. Definitions to Memorize (One-Liners for 2-Mark Questions)
1. Compound circuit — multi-actuator, single pump, individual flow control.
2. Combination circuit — superimposition of two or more basic circuit functions.
3. Regenerative circuit — recycles rod-end flow to cap-end for speed gain.
4. Flow divider — maintains fixed ratio between two actuator flows.
5. PLC — solid-state industrial controller with programmable memory.
6. SCADA — supervisory monitoring of geographically distributed assets.
7. DCS — distributed control of an entire process plant.
8. Fixed automation — dedicated equipment for one product.
9. Flexible automation — on-the-fly changeover without retooling.
10. Industry 4.0 — integration of cyber-physical systems, IoT, and AI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Cost Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET831 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Cost Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low Cost Automation (LCA)** is defined as the judicious and engineered use of **standardized, off-the-shelf, modular automation components** — such as mechanical linkages, pneumatic cylinders, hydraulic actuators, limit switches, timers, relays, sensors, and basic logic controllers — to mechanize or semi-automate a manufacturing process at a **fraction of the capital investment** required for a fully dedicated, hard-automated production line.

Mathematically, the philosophy of LCA can be expressed as:

$$
\text{LCA Philosophy} = f(\text{Standard Components}, \text{Modular Design}, \text{Minimal Custom Engineering}, \text{Optimal ROI})
$$

The objective is to achieve automation benefits — **consistency, productivity, safety, and quality** — without resorting to dedicated transfer lines, robotic cells, or CNC-heavy infrastructure, which typically require investments in the range of **₹10 Lakh – ₹1 Crore+**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** LCA is sometimes also referred to in industry as **"Rationalized Automation"** or **"Frugal Automation."** It occupies the middle ground between *manual labor* and *hard (fixed) automation*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Smart Kitchen Gadget" vs the "Industrial Catering Plant"**
>
> Imagine you run a small home-based bakery. You buy a **₹2,000 stand mixer** (a simple, standard, off-the-shelf tool) to knead dough. You have **mechanized** a task — saving your arms, getting consistent dough, and improving quality. This is **Low Cost Automation**.
>
> Now imagine you scale up to a 10,000-loaf-a-day industrial bakery. You install a **₹50 Lakh automated dough line** with conveyors, programmable proofers, and robotic loaders. This is **Hard Automation**.
>
> **LCA = The "Smart Gadget" Approach.** You take small, smart, standardized pieces — a clamp here, a sensor there, a pneumatic cylinder to push a part — and assemble them into a **semi-automatic workstation** that does a specific repetitive job efficiently, without rebuilding the whole factory.

### 1.3 Why Does LCA Exist? — The Justification

| Driving Factor | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **High cost of hard automation** | Dedicated fixtures, custom robots, and CNC systems are unaffordable for SMEs and job shops. |
| **Small/medium batch sizes** | Hard automation only pays off for very large production volumes (typically > 100,000 parts/year). |
| **Frequent product changes** | In dynamic markets, hard automation becomes obsolete fast. LCA can be **reconfigured** by swapping modules. |
| **Need to retain human flexibility** | Many operations still require human judgment (assembly of intricate parts). LCA assists the human rather than replacing them. |
| **Safety and ergonomics** | Automating the most hazardous or tiring sub-task (lifting, pressing, spraying) dramatically improves the workstation. |
| **Faster payback period** | Typical LCA investments pay back in **6–18 months**, versus 3–5 years for hard automation. |

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU Board Examinable)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Payback Period:** **1 to 3 years** (typical range for LCA projects)
> - **Return on Investment (ROI):** **> 30% per annum** is the KTU-designated benchmark for a "good" LCA project
> - **Capital Recovery Threshold:** LCA projects usually cost **< ₹10 Lakh** to qualify as "low cost"
> - **Target Production Volume:** Effective for batches between **5,000 and 100,000 units/year**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Building Blocks / Components of LCA

A Low Cost Automation system is assembled from **standard, readily-available building blocks**. These can be classified into five families:

#### A. Mechanical Building Blocks
- Linear bearings, guide rails, lead screws, ball screws
- Cams, linkages, camshafts, indexing tables
- Clamps, fixtures, jigs, pallet changers
- Power transmission elements: belts, chains, gears
- Material handling aids: chutes, slides, conveyors, vibratory bowl feeders

#### B. Pneumatic Building Blocks
- Double-acting and single-acting cylinders
- Rotary actuators, grippers, vacuum suction cups
- 5/2 and 3/2 way DCVs (Direction Control Valves)
- FRL unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) — supplies clean **6 bar (≈ 0.6 MPa)** compressed air
- Quick-exhaust valves, flow control valves, shuttle valves

#### C. Hydraulic Building Blocks (for higher force applications)
- Hydraulic cylinders (typically **up to 700 bar / 70 MPa**)
- Hydraulic power packs, DCVs, pressure relief valves
- Used in LCA where forces exceed **~ 5,000 N**, beyond pneumatic capability

#### D. Electrical / Electronic Building Blocks
- Limit switches, proximity sensors (inductive, capacitive, magnetic)
- Photoelectric sensors, encoders
- Relays, contactors, motor starters
- Timers (ON-delay, OFF-delay, cyclic)
- Counters, push buttons, selector switches, pilot lamps

#### E. Logic / Control Building Blocks (Pre-PLC era, still relevant for LCA)
- **Pneumatic Logic Circuits** (AND, OR, NOT gates built from air nozzles and flapper valves)
- **Relay Logic Ladder Diagrams**
- **Fluidic Logic** (no moving parts, uses jet deflection)
- Modern LCA may also use **mini-PLCs** or **Arduino/microcontroller boards** — but the spirit of LCA favors simplicity

### 2.2 Classification / Types of Low Cost Automation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Diagram Question Type:** Students must be able to draw and label the LCA hierarchy.

$$
\text{LCA Types} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Type I: Mechanization (no feedback)} \\
\text{Type II: Mechanization + Manual Feedback (operator triggers cycle)} \\
\text{Type III: Mechanization + Sensor Feedback (semi-automatic)} \\
\text{Type IV: Fully Programmed LCA (uses PLC / microcontroller)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.3 Steps in Implementing an LCA Project

1. **Identify the bottleneck / target operation** — the most tedious, hazardous, or inconsistent manual task.
2. **Analyze the operation** — sketch the cycle, list inputs/outputs, identify forces, strokes, and timings.
3. **Select the appropriate building blocks** — choose from mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, electrical.
4. **Design the sequence (logic)** — using relay ladder diagrams, pneumatic logic circuits, or PLC programming.
5. **Build the prototype** — assemble modular components on a frame.
6. **Test, debug, and optimize** — cycle time, ergonomics, safety interlocks.
7. **Document and deploy** — operator instructions, maintenance schedule.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Economic Justification of LCA

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Time to recover the initial cost from savings | **years** |
| 2 | $\text{ROI (\%)} = \left( \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings} - \text{Depreciation}}{\text{Investment}} \right) \times 100$ | Return on Investment — KTU benchmark: **> 30%** | **%** |
| 3 | $\text{Annual Savings} = (\text{Labor Cost Saved}) + (\text{Reject Cost Saved}) + (\text{Throughput Gain})$ | Total yearly benefit | **₹/year** |
| 4 | $\text{Break-even Volume} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost (LCA Investment)}}{\text{Savings per Unit}}$ | Minimum annual production to justify LCA | **units/year** |
| 5 | $\text{Cycle Time} = t_{\text{load}} + t_{\text{work}} + t_{\text{return}} + t_{\text{unload}}$ | Total time for one automated cycle | **seconds** |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{3600}{t_{\text{cycle}}} \times \eta$ | Parts produced per hour; $\eta$ = efficiency (typically **0.85**) | **parts/hour** |
| 7 | $\text{Compressed Air Consumption} = Q \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} \cdot n$ | Free air delivered per cycle; $n$ = strokes/cycle | **litres** |

> [!NOTE]
> **No `|` vertical bars used** in the table cells to preserve markdown formatting. Absolute values are expressed using the `\vert` operator.

### 2.5 Advantages of LCA (High-Yield for KTU Answers)

- **Low initial investment** (typically **₹50,000 – ₹10 Lakh**)
- **Short payback period** — usually **< 2 years**
- **Modular & reconfigurable** — components can be reused when product changes
- **Easy to maintain** — standard spares are readily available
- **Improves operator safety and ergonomics**
- **Enhances product quality** through consistency
- **Suitable for SMEs** (Small and Medium Enterprises) which dominate Indian manufacturing

### 2.6 Limitations of LCA

- Limited to **semi-automatic** or **single-station** operations
- Not suitable for **very high volume** production (where hard automation wins)
- Cycle times slower than dedicated hard automation
- Requires **skilled technicians** to assemble and troubleshoot
- Sensor reliability may be a bottleneck in dusty/harsh environments

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

LCA is extensively used in:
- **Automotive ancillaries** (small parts feeding, pressing, riveting)
- **Pharmaceutical packaging** (counting, capping, labeling)
- **Electronics assembly** (PCB insertion, soldering)
- **Food processing** (sorting, filling, sealing)
- **Foundries & forging** (handling hot parts — improving operator safety dramatically)

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Economic Justification for an LCA Project (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 12-Mark Style):**
A small manufacturing unit currently employs **3 workers** at a daily wage of **₹600 each** to perform a manual pressing operation. With Low Cost Automation, only **1 operator** (a supervisory role) is needed. The manual operation produces **200 parts/day** with a rejection rate of **6%**, while the LCA system produces **280 parts/day** with a rejection rate of **1.5%**. Each rejected part costs **₹40** in material wastage. The LCA system costs **₹4,00,000** to install, has an annual maintenance cost of **₹20,000**, and operates **300 days/year**.

Calculate:
1. Annual labor savings
2. Annual rejection cost savings
3. Net annual savings
4. Payback period
5. ROI

---

### Step 1: Annual Labor Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Workers before LCA} &= 3 \\
\text{Workers after LCA} &= 1 \\
\text{Workers saved} &= 3 - 1 = 2 \\
\text{Daily wage savings} &= 2 \times \text{₹}600 = \text{₹}1{,}200 \\
\text{Annual labor savings} &= 1{,}200 \times 300 = \text{₹}3{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Two workers are no longer needed on the pressing station. Their annual wages (₹600/day × 300 working days) constitute the direct labor saving.

---

### Step 2: Annual Rejection Cost Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rejected parts/day (manual)} &= 200 \times 0.06 = 12 \text{ parts/day} \\
\text{Rejected parts/day (LCA)} &= 280 \times 0.015 = 4.2 \text{ parts/day} \\
\text{Rejection reduction/day} &= 12 - 4.2 = 7.8 \text{ parts/day} \\
\text{Daily rejection savings} &= 7.8 \times \text{₹}40 = \text{₹}312 \\
\text{Annual rejection savings} &= 312 \times 300 = \text{₹}93{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** LCA reduces the defect rate from 6% to 1.5%. With higher throughput AND lower rejection rate, the saving comes from the *reduction in wastage per day* multiplied by working days.

---

### Step 3: Gross Annual Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Annual Savings} &= \text{Labor Savings} + \text{Rejection Savings} \\
&= \text{₹}3{,}60{,}000 + \text{₹}93{,}600 \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}53{,}600 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Both savings are real, recurring benefits that compound annually.

---

### Step 4: Net Annual Savings (after Maintenance Deduction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Annual Savings} &= \text{Gross Savings} - \text{Annual Maintenance} \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}53{,}600 - \text{₹}20{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}33{,}600 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Maintenance cost is an annual recurring expense and must be deducted from gross savings to get true net benefit.

---

### Step 5: Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback Period} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Net Annual Savings}} \\
&= \dfrac{\text{₹}4{,}00{,}000}{\text{₹}4{,}33{,}600} \\
&= 0.923 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 11 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is well within the KTU-benchmark range of "good" LCA projects (typically **< 2 years**). This single result justifies the project.

---

### Step 6: Return on Investment (ROI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation} &= \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{10 \text{ years (assumed asset life)}} = \text{₹}40{,}000 \\
\text{ROI} &= \left( \dfrac{\text{Net Savings} - \text{Depreciation}}{\text{Investment}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \dfrac{\text{₹}4{,}33{,}600 - \text{₹}40{,}000}{\text{₹}4{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \dfrac{3{,}93{,}600}{4{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 98.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** An ROI of **98.4%** is *excellent* — far above the KTU benchmark of **30%**. The investment is recovered in less than a year and continues to generate value.

---

### 3.2 Throughput Derivation

Given:
- $t_{\text{cycle}} = 15$ seconds (sum of load + work + return + unload)
- $\eta = 0.85$ (efficiency factor accounting for operator delay, sensor settling, etc.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical parts/hour} &= \dfrac{3600}{15} = 240 \text{ parts/hour} \\
\text{Actual Throughput} &= 240 \times 0.85 = 204 \text{ parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The factor of 3600 converts hours to seconds. The efficiency $\eta$ captures real-world losses (warm-up time, jams, operator hesitation, sensor misfires).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use LCA vs Hard Automation

| Parameter | Manual | **Low Cost Automation** | Hard Automation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | Very Low (< ₹50K) | **Moderate (₹50K – ₹10L)** | Very High (> ₹50L) |
| Production Volume (units/yr) | < 5,000 | **5,000 – 100,000** | > 100,000 |
| Product Variety | Very High | **Medium–High** | Very Low (dedicated) |
| Cycle Time | Slow | **Medium** | Very Fast |
| Flexibility | Maximum | **High (reconfigurable)** | Minimum |
| Operator Skill Required | Medium | **Low–Medium** | Low |
| Payback Period | N/A | **< 2 years** | 3–7 years |
| Suitable for SME? | Yes | **Yes — best fit** | Rarely |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *justify* LCA in an exam, always quote this decision matrix. Examiners reward students who show they understand **when** LCA is appropriate (not just **what** it is).

```mermaid
graph LR

    partB --> partC[Foot Switch Initiated Cycle]

```

> **Reading the diagram:** A textbook LCA application — a semi-automatic pneumatic pressing workstation. The operator loads the part manually, presses a foot switch (or two-hand press for safety), and the pneumatic cylinder performs the pressing at approximately **1,500 N** (using a bore of ~63 mm at 6 bar). A limit switch confirms stroke completion and reverses the cycle. The human operator retains final loading/unloading flexibility — a hallmark of LCA.
