## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

#### **Q1. Define Low Cost Automation. List any four of its building blocks.** *[3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**
Low Cost Automation (LCA) is the use of standard, modular, off-the-shelf components — mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, electrical, and electronic — to partially or fully automate a manufacturing operation at a minimum capital investment, typically achieving payback within **1–2 years**.

**Four Building Blocks:**
1. **Mechanical:** Cams, linkages, slides, fixtures, vibratory feeders.
2. **Pneumatic:** Cylinders, 5/2 DCVs, FRL unit, grippers.
3. **Electrical:** Limit switches, relays, timers, contactors.
4. **Electronic/Logic:** Proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors, mini-PLCs.

---

#### **Q2. Mention any three situations where Low Cost Automation is preferred over Hard Automation.** *[3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Small to medium batch sizes** (typically 5,000 to 100,000 units/year), where hard automation does not justify its cost.
2. **Frequent product changes**, requiring the workstation to be reconfigured — LCA components can be reused, unlike dedicated hard automation lines.
3. **Limited capital availability** in SMEs, where a payback period of < 2 years is essential for survival.
4. **(Optional 4th)** Operations requiring human skill/dexterity that cannot be fully replaced by machines.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024, End Semester Exam Pattern]**
> *Module Internal Choice: Answer either Question A OR Question B.*

---

#### **✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(A). (a)** Explain the concept of Low Cost Automation with a neat block diagram of its building blocks. Discuss **mechanical**, **pneumatic**, and **electrical** building blocks in detail. **[*7 Marks*]**

**(b)** A manual drilling operation uses **2 workers** at a wage of ₹500/day each. After installing an LCA jig with a pneumatic feed cylinder, only **1 worker** is required. Production increases from **150 parts/day to 250 parts/day**, and rejection drops from **5% to 1%**. Each rejected part costs ₹30. The LCA system costs ₹3,00,000 with annual maintenance of ₹15,000. The unit operates **300 days/year**.

**Calculate:**
(i) Annual labor savings
(ii) Annual rejection savings
(iii) Payback period
(iv) ROI (assume 10-year asset life) **[*7 Marks*]**

##### 🟢 Model Solution for Q(A)

**(a) Concept and Building Blocks:** *[7 Marks]*

**Concept:** Low Cost Automation (LCA) is the engineered use of standardized, modular building blocks to mechanize or semi-automate industrial operations economically, with typical payback periods of 1–2 years and investments under ₹10 Lakh.

**Block Diagram (described):**
- **Energy Input** → Mechanical / Pneumatic / Hydraulic / Electrical
- **Actuators** → Cylinders, motors, grippers
- **Sensors** → Limit, proximity, photoelectric
- **Logic Control** → Relays, pneumatic logic, mini-PLC
- **Output** → Workpiece operation
- **Feedback** → To operator / next station

**Mechanical Building Blocks:**
- Cams and cam followers for converting rotary motion into linear/oscillating motion.
- Linkages (four-bar, slider-crank) for path generation.
- Indexing tables for intermittent rotary indexing.
- Linear guides and ball screws for precision linear motion.
- Vibratory bowl feeders for part orientation and feeding.

**Pneumatic Building Blocks:**
- Double-acting cylinders (bore sizes 25–100 mm typical) producing forces up to ~5,000 N at **6 bar**.
- 5/2 and 3/2 DCVs for direction control.
- FRL unit ensuring clean, regulated, lubricated air.
- Flow control valves for speed regulation (meter-in / meter-out).

**Electrical Building Blocks:**
- Limit switches for end-of-stroke detection.
- Proximity sensors (inductive for metals, capacitive for non-metals).
- Relays (8-pin, 11-pin) for logic implementation.
- Timers (ON-delay, OFF-delay) for sequence timing.
- Contactors and motor starters for motor control.

---

**(b) Economic Calculation:** *[7 Marks]*

**(i) Annual Labor Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Workers saved} &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
\text{Daily labor savings} &= 1 \times \text{₹}500 = \text{₹}500 \\
\text{Annual labor savings} &= 500 \times 300 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual Rejection Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rejected/day (manual)} &= 150 \times 0.05 = 7.5 \\
\text{Rejected/day (LCA)} &= 250 \times 0.01 = 2.5 \\
\text{Rejection reduction/day} &= 7.5 - 2.5 = 5 \\
\text{Daily rejection savings} &= 5 \times \text{₹}30 = \text{₹}150 \\
\text{Annual rejection savings} &= 150 \times 300 = \text{₹}45{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Payback Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Savings} &= \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000 + \text{₹}45{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}95{,}000 \\
\text{Net Savings} &= 1{,}95{,}000 - 15{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Payback} &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 1.67 \text{ years} \approx 20 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) ROI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation} &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{₹}30{,}000 \\
\text{ROI} &= \left( \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Payback of 1.67 years and ROI of 50% — both within KTU "good project" benchmarks.

---

#### **✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice if Question A is skipped)*

**Q(B). (a)** Compare Low Cost Automation with Hard Automation on at least **six parameters**. Justify why LCA is the preferred choice for the **Indian SME manufacturing sector**. **[*7 Marks*]**

**(b)** A semi-automatic pneumatic stamping station uses a double-acting cylinder of bore **50 mm** and stroke **100 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar**, and the cylinder operates at **30 cycles/minute**. Calculate:

(i) Theoretical pushing force of the cylinder.
(ii) Air consumption per cycle (in litres, free air).
(iii) Theoretical hourly throughput (assume $\eta = 0.85$). **[*7 Marks*]**

##### 🟢 Model Solution for Q(B)

**(a) Comparison LCA vs Hard Automation:** *[7 Marks]*

| Parameter | Low Cost Automation | Hard Automation |
|---|---|---|
| Initial cost | ₹50K – ₹10 Lakh | > ₹50 Lakh (often > ₹1 Cr) |
| Production volume | 5,000 – 100,000 units/yr | > 100,000 units/yr |
| Flexibility | High (reconfigurable) | Very low (dedicated) |
| Payback period | < 2 years | 3–7 years |
| Skill required to set up | Moderate (technician level) | High (automation engineer) |
| Maintenance | Easy (standard spares) | Complex (proprietary spares) |
| Best fit | SMEs, job shops, varied batches | Mass production of single product |

**Justification for Indian SME Sector:**
- Indian SMEs have **limited capital** — they cannot afford crores for hard automation.
- Indian market demands **product variety** — LCA can be reconfigured faster.
- **Skilled but affordable labor** is available — LCA augments rather than replaces humans.
- **Short payback** fits Indian business cash-flow cycles.
- Government initiatives (e.g., **Make in India**, **ZED Certification**) actively promote LCA-style modernization.

---

**(b) Pneumatic Cylinder Calculations:** *[7 Marks]*

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Stroke $L = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- Pressure $P = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Cycles/min $= 30$
- $\eta = 0.85$

**(i) Theoretical Pushing Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
F &= P \times A = (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.963 \times 10^{-3}) \\
F &= 1178 \text{ N} \approx 1.18 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Air Consumption per Cycle (Free Air):**

Volume of one stroke (forward + return):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{cycle}} &= 2 \times A \times L = 2 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 \\
&= 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
&= 0.3927 \text{ litres per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical Hourly Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{cycle}} &= \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{30 \text{ cycles}} = 2 \text{ s/cycle} \\
\text{Theoretical parts/hr} &= \dfrac{3600}{2} = 1800 \text{ parts/hr} \\
\text{Actual throughput} &= 1800 \times 0.85 = 1530 \text{ parts/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **1. Confusing LCA with "low-tech" or "poor-quality" automation.**
> Examiners *specifically deduct* marks if students describe LCA as inferior or "cheap." LCA is **strategically designed** to be cost-effective — it uses **standard premium components**, not substandard ones.
>
> **2. Forgetting to subtract annual maintenance before computing Payback/ROI.**
> Always calculate **Net Savings = Gross Savings − Annual Maintenance** before applying the formula. This is the **#1 mistake** KTU examiners flag.
>
> **3. Wrong units in pneumatic cylinder problems.**
> Pressure must be in **Pa (N/m²)** when multiplied by area in **m²** to get force in **N**. Converting bar to Pa: multiply by **$10^5$**.
>
> **4. Missing the stroke factor (×2) in air consumption.**
> For a double-acting cylinder, both extension and retraction consume air. Total volume = **$2 \times A \times L$**.
>
> **5. Not labeling the block diagram.**
> If asked to draw the LCA building blocks, **label every block AND show arrows for signal/energy flow direction**. Unlabeled diagrams fetch 0 marks even if drawn correctly.
>
> **6. Quoting payback without units.**
> "1.67" is meaningless. Write "**1.67 years ≈ 20 months**" — examiners reward unit clarity.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Low Cost Automation (LCA)** uses **standard, modular, off-the-shelf components** to semi-automate processes economically.
- ✅ LCA sits **between manual labor and hard automation** on the automation hierarchy.
- ✅ **Investment range:** ₹50,000 to ₹10 Lakh | **Payback:** < 2 years | **ROI benchmark:** > 30%.
- ✅ **Five families of building blocks:** Mechanical, Pneumatic, Hydraulic, Electrical, Electronic/Logic.
- ✅ **Pneumatic pressure:** typically **6 bar**; **Hydraulic pressure:** up to **700 bar** — choose hydraulic only when force exceeds ~5,000 N.
- ✅ **Cylinder force formula:** $F = P \times A = P \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$.
- ✅ **Air consumption per cycle (double-acting):** $V = 2 \times A \times L$.
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** $\text{Actual} = \dfrac{3600}{t_{\text{cycle}}} \times \eta$ with $\eta \approx 0.85$.
- ✅ **Payback Period = Total Investment ÷ Net Annual Savings.**
- ✅ **ROI = [(Net Savings − Depreciation) / Investment] × 100.**
- ✅ LCA is **best suited for SMEs**, **batch sizes 5K–100K**, and **frequent product changes**.
- ✅ Always **subtract maintenance cost** before computing payback — examiners WILL check.
- ✅ **Label every block** in diagrams — credit is given per labeled component, not per drawing.
- ✅ LCA is **NOT inferior to hard automation** — it is a **strategic choice** for the right context.
